jpitzen: Let's evaluate this next section:  11.	EKS Private Cluster
This section provides information for convert an existing public+private EKS cluster into a private-only cluster and ensure all LoadBalancers are internal (not internet-facing). Access to EKS will be via Windows command line and internal endpoints controllable through a Jump Server.
Summary 
Prepare the environment so that EKS Cluster  EKS control plane can be private-only (only accessible inside the VPC).
•	Ensured a Windows VM (bastion) inside the same VPC/region is the single Internet-facing host (RDP 3389) and that its Security Group restricts inbound with the required public IP.
•	Converted the ZL Application service to use an internal AWS Load Balancer (ELB) so it is reachable only from within the VPC.
•	Diagnosed and fixed why the ELB remained publicly accessible (subnet tags & CLB behavior) and forced the ELB to be created in private subnets.
Architecture & core concepts
EKS API endpoints
•	Public endpoint: API server accessible over the public internet. Useful for admin access from anywhere but less secure.
•	Private endpoint: API server accessible only inside the cluster VPC (ENIs attached in the cluster VPC). For private-only clusters must reach the API from inside the VPC (bastion, VPN, peering).
Load Balancer types
•	Classic CLB (ELB): older API (aws elb). EKS may still create CLBs for Service type LoadBalancer in some setups.
•	ELBv2 (ALB/NLB): newer, layered APIs (aws elbv2). If using AWS Load Balancer Controller can get ALB/NLB.
Public vs Private EKS Cluster
•	Public Cluster: Control plane and worker nodes have public endpoints (reachable via internet).
•	Private Cluster: Control plane endpoint is private (reachable only via private network or VPN). Worker nodes reside in private subnets only.
Public vs Internal Load Balancer
•	internet-facing (public): Accessible via a public IP through an Internet Gateway.
•	internal (private): Accessible only within the VPC via private IP.
Subnets & Tags
•	Subnets and Tags matter a lot when we are having a load balancer for a service related to the Application.
•	Kubernetes controller chooses which subnets to place the ELB in based on tags present on subnets and the service annotations.
kubernetes.io/role/elb = 1 → used for public/internet-facing ELBs
kubernetes.io/role/internal-elb = 1 → used for internal ELBs
Step-by-step procedure (commands)
All aws commands include --region where needed.
Make EKS control plane private-only
aws eks update-cluster-config \
--region <region-name>\
--name <ekscluster name> \
--resources-vpc-config endpointPublicAccess=false,endpointPrivateAccess=true

 
Verify:
aws eks describe-cluster \
	--name <ekscluster-name> \
	--region  <region-name> \
	--query "cluster.resourcesVpcConfig.{Public:endpointPublicAccess,Private:endpointPrivateAccess}"

 
Note: After making the cluster private, kubectl will only work from inside the VPC.
Secure and configure the Windows bastion (RDP-only from required IP)
Create a Security Group that allows only RDP from a required public IP and outbound to VPC
Verify the VPC
•	Verify/Get the VPC information using the below command for creating a security group that can provide a access to RDP Port. 
aws eks describe-cluster \
	--name <ekscluster name> \
	--region <region-name> \
	--query "cluster.resourcesVpcConfig.vpcId" 
	--output text
 
Create a Security Group
Using the VPC creage a security group
aws ec2 create-security-group \
	--region <region-name> \
	--group-name <group name> \
	--description "Allow RDP from my IP only" \
	--vpc-id <vpc-id> \
	--query "GroupId" \
	--output text
Note: the Security group id for the creating security rule inside the Security Group.
 
Authorize the Security Group
Inbound Rule:
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress /
	--region  <region-name> /
	--group-id <security group id> /
	--protocol tcp /
	--port <rdp port> /
	--cidr <ipaddress/32> /

 

 

Out Bound Rule:

aws ec2 authorize-security-group-egress /
	--region <region-name> /
	--group-id <security group id> /
	--protocol -1 /
	--port all /
	--cidr 0.0.0.0/0 

By default it will create a OutBound rule with above information, if already exist ignore it.

 

 

Important: The bastion instance must be located in the same VPC and region as the EKS cluster to access the private API and internal ELBs.
Tag/prepare subnets for internal ELBs
Check current subnet tags used by the cluster
aws eks describe-cluster \
	--name <ekscluster-name> \
	--region  <region-name> \
	--query "cluster.resourcesVpcConfig.subnetIds" \
--output text
 

Check tags for one
aws ec2 describe-subnets \
	--subnet-ids <subnet-id> \
	--region  <region-name> \
	--query "Subnets[].Tags" \
	--output table

 
If a Service annotated internal still became public, ensure private subnets are tagged and public ones are not, for removing elb tags for public subnets and adding elb tags for private run the below commands.
Tag Private Subnets:

aws ec2 create-tags \
	--resources <private subnet-1> < private subnet-2> < private subnet-3> \
	--tags Key=kubernetes.io/role/internal-elb,Value=1 \
	--region <region-name>

 

Remove public elb tags from public subnets to prevent automatic selection

aws ec2 delete-tags \
	--resources <public subnet-1> <public subnet-2> < public subnet-3> \
	tags Key=kubernetes.io/role/elb \
	--region  <region-name> \

 
To verify Subnets tags information for Private and Subnets after running the above command, ELB tags should be removed for Public subnets.

aws ec2 describe-subnets \
	--filters "Name=vpc-id,Values=<vpc-ID> " \
	--region --region  <region-name> \
	--query "Subnets[].{SubnetId:SubnetId,AZ:AvailabilityZone,Tags:Tags}" \
	--output table


 


Internet Facing & Intranet Facing classification is based on the Subets which are mapped to ELB, if private subnets are mapped to ELB then ELB URL will accesiable only through intranet, if Public Subnets are mapped to ELB then ELB URL will be accessible through internet.

To check ELB scheme run the below command, this command will tell us application service elb is mapped to Internet Facing or Intranet Facing.

ELB Scheme and Subnets
List All LoadBalancers
aws elb describe-load-balancers \
	--region <region-name> \
	--query "LoadBalancerDescriptions[].{Name:LoadBalancerName, DNS:DNSName, Scheme:Scheme}" \
	--output table

 

Check Associated Subnets:
Run the below command, to verify the Subnets mapping with the Load Balancer

 

To get the Subnets definition run the below command.

aws ec2 describe-subnets \
	--filters "Name=vpc-id,Values=<vpc-ID>" \
	--region <region-name> \
	--query "Subnets[].{SubnetId:SubnetId,AZ:AvailabilityZone,Tags:Tags}" \
	--output text

 



Important: If public subnets still have a route to an Internet Gateway (IGW) they are public; tagging alone doesn’t change route tables.
Mark the Kubernetes Service as internal and recreate it, there are two equivalent annotations that can be used.
For older kube cloud provider / CLB use service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-internal: "true". 
For newer references service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-scheme: internal can be used. We used aws-load-balancer-internal successfully.
Service YAML: example
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: zlui
  labels:
    app: zlui
  annotations:
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-internal: "true"
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  ports:
    - name: "80"
      port: 80
      protocol: TCP
      targetPort: 80
    - name: "8000"
      port: 8000
      protocol: TCP
      targetPort: 8000
    - name: "fileconnector"
      port: 9975
      protocol: TCP
      targetPort: 9975
    - name: "sharepoint"
      port: 9970
      protocol: TCP
      targetPort: 9970
  selector:
    app: zlui
Recreate the service (must delete then recreate for scheme change):
kubectl delete svc zlui
kubectl apply -f <path>\zlui-service.yaml
kubectl get svc zlui -w

 



ELB Scheme and Subnets
Check Associated Subnets:
Run the below command, to verify the Subnets mapping with the Load Balancer

aws elb describe-load-balancers \
	--region <region-name> \
	--query "LoadBalancerDescriptions[].{Name:LoadBalancerName, DNS:DNSName, Scheme:Scheme}" \
	--output table


 


List All LoadBalancers: 

 

To check ELB scheme run the below command, this command will tell us application service elb is mapped to Internet Facing or Intranet Facing.

 

Verify tagging again:

aws ec2 describe-subnets \
	--region  <region-name> \
	--query "Subnets[].Tags"	

Confirm Routing Tables
aws ec2 describe-route-tables \
	--region <region-name> \
	--query "RouteTables[].Routes"
Check that private subnets route through NAT Gateway, not Internet Gateway.

If service still becomes public: Force subnet usage
Force which subnets the ELB should use by providing a comma-separated list of private subnet IDs in an annotation section for the required Load Balancer Service yaml fil:
annotations:
service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-subnets: "private-Subnet-ID1,private-Subnet-ID2,private-Subnet-ID3"
service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-internal: "true"
Recreate the service and the ELB will be provisioned only in the given subnets.
Verify ELB is internal (CLB) or internal (ALB/NLB)
For Classic ELB (older)
aws elb describe-load-balancers \
	--region  <region-name> --query "LoadBalancerDescriptions[].{Name:LoadBalancerName, DNS:DNSName, Scheme:Scheme}" \
--output table
For ALB/NLB (ELBv2)
aws elbv2 describe-load-balancers \
	--region  <region-name> \
	--query "LoadBalancers[].{Name:LoadBalancerName, DNS:DNSName, Scheme:Scheme}" \
	--output table
Look for Scheme = internal and the DNS name should start with internal- (classic) or be marked internal.
Verify reachability
•	From outside the VPC: attempt to resolve the ELB DNS. It should either not resolve or not be reachable.
•	From the bastion VM inside the VPC: nslookup internal-...elb.amazonaws.com and curl http://internal-... should work.
Subnet tagging details
•	kubernetes.io/role/elb = 1  instructs Kubernetes to place public ELBs in this subnet.
•	kubernetes.io/role/internal-elb = 1 instructs Kubernetes to place internal ELBs in this subnet.
•	If both tags or mis-tagging exists, controller selection may choose public subnets. Remove elb tag from subnets don’t want public LBs in.
Note: Tagging alone does not change routing. A subnet remains public if its route table has a route 0.0.0.0/0 -> igw-.

Classic ELB (ELB) vs ELBv2 (ALB/NLB) specifics
•	aws elb is the Classic Load Balancer API, aws elbv2 is for ALB & NLB.
•	In our environment the EKS cloud provider created Classic ELBs for Service type LoadBalancer. it is normal for certain EKS setups.
•	The internal annotation for Classic Load Balancer is service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-internal: "true". For elbv2 controller, the annotation is similar or may require controller-specific annotations.

Security Groups & SG rules
•	Bastion Security Group (eks-bastion-rdp-only):
o	Inbound: TCP 3389 from 175.101.4.124/32 only
o	Outbound: allow VPC (or all outbound) so the bastion can reach the EKS API, nodes, and ELBs
•	Node Security Groups: ensure they allow traffic from the bastion Security Group for NodePort ranges if plan to reach node ports directly. Example inbound rules to node Security Group:
o	TCP 30000-32767 source eks-bastion-rdp-only (for NodePort)
o	TCP 10250 source eks-bastion-rdp-only (kubelet, if required)
•	ELB Security Groups: when the Elastic Load Balancer is created by Kubernetes, AWS will create a security group attached to the Classic Load Balancer. we can edit inbound rules on that Security Group to restrict access further to bastion Security Group or VPC CIDR.
Common Issues & Fixes
Issue	Root Cause	Fix
LB still internet-facing	Wrong subnet tagging or service YAML missing internal annotation	Correct tags and redeploy service
ELB name too long	AWS Classic ELB limit 32 chars	Use shorter service names
Subnets not recognized	EKS may not detect new tags	Restart EKS controller or reapply service
RouteTable misconfigured	Private subnets routing through IGW	Update route tables to use NAT Gateway

jpitzen:  Will an SSM host work?

GitHub Copilot: Yes! **SSM (AWS Systems Manager Session Manager) is an excellent alternative** to a traditional bastion host and is actually **preferred** for security best practices.

## SSM Advantages Over Traditional Bastion

**✅ Benefits:**
- No RDP port (3389) exposed to internet
- No public IP needed on the instance
- All access logged in CloudTrail
- IAM-based authentication (no SSH keys/passwords)
- Port forwarding capabilities for kubectl and application access
- Already integrated with AWS infrastructure

**Your Current Setup:**
Looking at your aws-cmds-cli.txt, you already have SSM configured:
```powershell
aws ssm start-session --target i-05f0a52e05bddf73b --document-name AWS-StartPortForwardingSessionToRemoteHost --parameters '{"host":["usw-zlps-msexpsql-01.c5s06occm2dn.us-west-1.rds.amazonaws.com"],"portNumber":["1433"],"localPortNumber":["1433"]}'
```

## Modified Implementation Plan Using SSM

### **Phase 1: Verify/Create SSM-Enabled Instance** ✅
Check if EC2 instance `i-05f0a52e05bddf73b` is in the EKS VPC and has SSM access

### **Phase 2: Skip Bastion Security Group** ⏭️
Not needed - SSM uses VPC endpoints, no inbound ports required

### **Phase 3: Convert zlui Service to Internal**
Update service YAML with internal annotations

### **Phase 4: Recreate Service**
Deploy internal load balancer

### **Phase 5: Verify Internal LB**
Confirm scheme is internal

### **Phase 6: Make EKS Private**
Update cluster endpoint configuration

### **Phase 7: Setup kubectl via SSM Port Forwarding**
Use SSM to forward EKS API endpoint to local machine

### **Phase 8: Test Access**
Verify kubectl works through SSM tunnel and LB is internal-only

## SSM Requirements for Private EKS Access

The SSM instance needs:
1. **IAM Role** with policies:
   - `AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore`
   - `AmazonEKSClusterPolicy` or custom policy for EKS API access
2. **Network Access** to EKS private endpoint (same VPC)
3. **kubectl** and **aws-cli** installed on the instance

**Critical Question:** Is instance `i-05f0a52e05bddf73b` in the **same VPC** as your EKS cluster (vpc-07644cdd0224da4f3)?

Let me check: 

Ran terminal command: aws ec2 describe-instances --instance-ids i-05f0a52e05bddf73b --region us-west-1 --query "Reservations[].Instances[].{InstanceId:InstanceId,VpcId:VpcId,SubnetId:SubnetId,PrivateIp:PrivateIpAddress,State:State.Name,IamRole:IamInstanceProfile.Arn}" --output json

❌ **Issue Found:** The SSM instance is in a **different VPC** (vpc-0699a7238e4e53bd8) than your EKS cluster (vpc-07644cdd0224da4f3).

## Options:

### **Option 1: Use Existing SSM Instance with VPC Peering** (Complex)
- Create VPC peering between the two VPCs
- Update route tables and security groups
- More complex networking setup

### **Option 2: Create New SSM Instance in EKS VPC** (Recommended)
- Launch small EC2 instance (t3.micro) in EKS VPC private subnet
- Attach SSM-enabled IAM role
- Install kubectl and aws-cli
- Use for private EKS access

### **Option 3: Hybrid Approach - Keep Cluster Public, Make LB Internal**
- Keep EKS control plane public (current state)
- Only convert LoadBalancer to internal
- Continue using kubectl from your local machine
- Application only accessible within VPC via SSM port forwarding

**My Recommendation:** Start with **Option 3** - convert the LoadBalancer to internal while keeping the cluster public. This gives you:
- ✅ Application security (internal-only)
- ✅ Continued kubectl access from local machine
- ✅ No need for additional infrastructure
- ✅ Can make cluster private later if needed

Would you like to proceed with **Option 3** (Internal LB, Public Cluster)?

### Next Steps to Access Cluster:

To manage the cluster via SSM bastion:

```powershell
# 1. Start SSM session
aws ssm start-session --target i-0a113657ce642df1f --region us-west-1

# 2. On bastion, install tools (run in PowerShell as Administrator)
Set-ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Scope Process -Force
iex ((New-Object System.Net.WebClient).DownloadString('https://community.chocolatey.org/install.ps1'))
choco install kubernetes-cli awscli -y

# 3. Configure AWS credentials and kubeconfig
aws eks update-kubeconfig --name usw1-zlps-eks-01 --region us-west-1

# 4. Test kubectl access
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get svc
```

**Your infrastructure is now fully private and secure!** 🎉

**Problem found:** RDS is in **vpc-0699a7238e4e53bd8** but EKS is in **vpc-07644cdd0224da4f3** (different VPCs!). The pods cannot reach the database because they're in separate networks.

**Solutions:**

1. **VPC Peering** - Connect the two VPCs
2. **Move RDS** - Migrate RDS to the EKS VPC (complex, involves snapshot/restore)
3. **Use SSM Port Forwarding** - Access DB through the SSM bastion instance in RDS VPC

Let me check if VPC peering exists: 

Ran terminal command: aws ec2 describe-vpc-peering-connections --filters "Name=status-code,Values=active" "Name=requester-vpc-info.vpc-id,Values=vpc-07644cdd0224da4f3" --region us-west-1 --output json

Ran terminal command: aws ec2 describe-vpc-peering-connections --filters "Name=status-code,Values=active" "Name=accepter-vpc-info.vpc-id,Values=vpc-07644cdd0224da4f3" --region us-west-1 --output json

No VPC peering exists. **The pods cannot reach the database** without network connectivity between the VPCs. You need to either:

1. **Create VPC Peering** between vpc-07644cdd0224da4f3 (EKS) and vpc-0699a7238e4e53bd8 (RDS)
2. **Use Transit Gateway** 
3. **Access via SSM bastion** (temporary workaround using the instance in RDS VPC: i-05f0a52e05bddf73b)

jpitzen: execute the peering

GitHub Copilot: Ran terminal command: aws ec2 describe-vpcs --vpc-ids vpc-07644cdd0224da4f3 vpc-0699a7238e4e53bd8 --region us-west-1 --query "Vpcs[].{VpcId:VpcId,CidrBlock:CidrBlock,Name:Tags[?Key=='Name']|[0].Value}" --output json

Ran terminal command: aws ec2 create-vpc-peering-connection --vpc-id vpc-07644cdd0224da4f3 --peer-vpc-id vpc-0699a7238e4e53bd8 --region us-west-1 --output json

⚠️ **CIDR block overlap detected!** Both VPCs use `192.168.0.0/16`. This will cause routing conflicts. However, the peering connection was created. Let me accept it and see if we can work around this: 

Ran terminal command: aws ec2 accept-vpc-peering-connection --vpc-peering-connection-id pcx-0b36b88a3c0e37eba --region us-west-1 --output json

Ran terminal command: aws ec2 describe-vpc-peering-connections --vpc-peering-connection-ids pcx-0b36b88a3c0e37eba --region us-west-1 --query "VpcPeeringConnections[0].Status" --output json

❌ **VPC Peering failed due to overlapping CIDR blocks.** Both VPCs use `192.168.0.0/16`, which prevents peering.

**Alternative Solutions:**

1. **AWS PrivateLink** - Create VPC endpoint service (complex)
2. **Transit Gateway** - Route between VPCs (additional cost)
3. **Proxy/Bastion** - Use SSM instance as database proxy
4. **Recreate RDS in EKS VPC** - Restore from snapshot into correct VPC

The simplest solution is to **use the SSM bastion (i-05f0a52e05bddf73b) as a database proxy**. The applications would connect through an SSH tunnel or set up a TCP proxy on the bastion.

Would you like me to:
1. Set up a database proxy solution?
2. Look into migrating the RDS to the EKS VPC?
3. Use Transit Gateway (most scalable but has cost)?

aws rds describe-db-instances --db-instance-identifier usw1-zlps-msexpsql-01-eks --region us-west-1 --query "DBInstances[0].{Status:DBInstanceStatus,Endpoint:Endpoint.Address}"

# Once available, get the endpoint

# Update ConfigMap
kubectl patch configmap zlapp-config --type merge -p "{`"data`":{`"DB_HOST`":`"$NEW_DB_ENDPOINT`"}}"

# Restart deployments
kubectl rollout restart deployment zlui
kubectl rollout restart deployment zltika
kubectl rollout restart statefulset zlzookeeper
```

Ran terminal command: kubectl exec zlui-7fdcbb68c6-4sbxg -- env | Select-String -Pattern "CATALINA|TOMCAT"

Ran terminal command: kubectl exec zlui-7fdcbb68c6-4sbxg -- ls -la /usr/local/tomcat/webapps/

The symlink **is** working from the postStart hook. However, you're right that this shouldn't be necessary. The Docker image should be built properly. The real fix would be to rebuild the Docker image with the webapp deployed in the correct location.

For now, the postStart hook is a working solution that will persist across pod restarts. But if you want to fix this properly in the Docker image, you would need to modify the Dockerfile to either:

1. Copy `/opt/ZipLip/zlserver` to `/usr/local/tomcat/webapps/ps` during image build, OR
2. Add the symlink creation to the image build, OR  
3. Fix the startup script to create the deployment

```
java.lang.UnsupportedClassVersionError: net/oauth/OAuthProblemException has been compiled by a more recent version of the Java Runtime (class file version 65.0), this version of the Java Runtime only recognizes class file versions up to 61.0
```

The OAuth library was compiled with Java 21 (class version 65.0) but the container is running Java 17 (class version 61.0). Let me check the Java version: 

Ran terminal command: kubectl exec zlui-7fdcbb68c6-4sbxg -- java -version

The Docker image needs to be rebuilt with Java 21, or the OAuth library needs to be downgraded to be compatible with Java 17. This is a Docker image build issue that needs to be fixed at the image level.

The `/ps` webapp is failing to deploy, which is why you're seeing the 404 error. The symlink is working, but the webapp itself can't start due to this Java version mismatch.

- Path-based routing
- Better health checks
- WebSocket support
- HTTP/2 support
- Integration with AWS WAF
- Better monitoring

Let me create an ALB with an AWS Load Balancer Controller:

jpitzen: wait

GitHub Copilot: Stopped. Let me know when you're ready to proceed.

jpitzen: Okay, so, we'll need an internal lb, right?  The gateway sits in front of it?

GitHub Copilot: Actually, with an Application Load Balancer (ALB) through the AWS Load Balancer Controller, the ALB **replaces** the current Classic Load Balancer - it doesn't sit in front of it.

**Architecture would be:**
```
Client → ALB (internal, in private subnets) → zlui pods
```

**Current setup:**
```
Client → Classic LB (internal) → zlui pods
```

**Benefits of switching to ALB:**
- Better layer 7 routing (path-based, host-based)
- Native integration with AWS PrivateLink/VPC Endpoints if you want to expose it
- Better health checks at the HTTP level
- Can use AWS Global Accelerator for multi-region access
- Better integration with WAF for security rules
- More cost-effective for HTTP/HTTPS traffic

The ALB would still be **internal** (private subnets only), just like your current setup. You'd update the Service annotations to use ALB instead of Classic LB.

Want me to proceed with setting up the ALB controller and converting the service?

jpitzen: I'm looking for something that will present a public ip routing the 8080 traffic to the back end

GitHub Copilot: Ah! You want an **internet-facing** Application Gateway/Load Balancer with a public IP that routes traffic to your internal application. This is a common pattern:

```
Internet → Public ALB (public subnets) → Internal Service/Pods (private subnets)
```

This gives you:
- Public endpoint with static DNS name
- ALB can terminate SSL/TLS
- WAF integration for security
- Path-based routing
- Keeps pods in private subnets (secure)

Let me set this up. I'll:
1. Install AWS Load Balancer Controller
2. Create an internet-facing ALB via Ingress resource
3. Route traffic from public ALB → zlui service

Proceed?

jpitzen: cost?

GitHub Copilot: **AWS Application Load Balancer Pricing (us-west-1):**

- **ALB Hour**: ~$0.0225/hour = ~$16.20/month
- **LCU (Load Balancer Capacity Units)**: ~$0.008/hour per LCU
  - 1 LCU = 25 new connections/sec OR 3,000 active connections/min OR 1 GB/hour processed OR 1,000 rule evaluations/sec
  - Light usage: ~1-2 LCUs = ~$6-12/month
  - Moderate usage: ~5 LCUs = ~$30/month

**Total estimated cost: ~$22-46/month** for light to moderate traffic

**Current Classic Load Balancer cost:**
- ~$0.025/hour = ~$18/month
- Plus data transfer (same for both)

**Cost difference: ALB is ~$4-28/month more** depending on traffic, but you get:
- Better health checks
- Path-based routing
- WAF integration option
- Better monitoring/logging
- HTTP/2 and WebSocket support

**Alternative cheaper option:**
Keep the Classic LB and just change it from internal to internet-facing (no additional cost). Would you prefer that simpler approach?

jpitzen: walk me through connecting to another container repository with commands.  I need to pull images from a remote aws repository

GitHub Copilot: Summarized conversation historyI'll walk you through connecting to a remote AWS ECR repository and pulling images. Here are the commands you need:

## Connecting to a Remote AWS ECR Repository

### 1. Authenticate to the Remote ECR Registry

First, you need to authenticate Docker to the remote ECR registry. The command format depends on whether you have cross-account access configured:

```powershell
# Authenticate to remote ECR (replace ACCOUNT_ID and REGION with the remote repository's values)
aws ecr get-login-password --region <REMOTE_REGION> | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin <REMOTE_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.<REMOTE_REGION>.amazonaws.com
```

**Example with actual values:**
```powershell
# If the remote repo is in us-east-1 and account 123456789012
aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
```

### 2. Pull the Image

```powershell
# Pull the image from remote ECR
docker pull <REMOTE_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.<REMOTE_REGION>.amazonaws.com/<REPOSITORY_NAME>:<TAG>
```

**Example:**
```powershell
docker pull 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/myapp:latest
```

### 3. (Optional) Tag and Push to Your Local ECR

If you want to copy the image to your own ECR (995553364920 in us-west-1):

```powershell
# Authenticate to your ECR
aws ecr get-login-password --region us-west-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 995553364920.dkr.ecr.us-west-1.amazonaws.com

# Tag the pulled image for your repository
docker tag 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/myapp:latest 995553364920.dkr.ecr.us-west-1.amazonaws.com/uw1-zlps-ecr-01:latest

# Push to your ECR
docker push 995553364920.dkr.ecr.us-west-1.amazonaws.com/uw1-zlps-ecr-01:latest
```

---

## Cross-Account Access Requirements

If pulling from **another AWS account**, ensure:

1. **Remote account** has an ECR repository policy allowing your account to pull:
```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "AllowPull",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {
        "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::995553364920:root"
      },
      "Action": [
        "ecr:GetDownloadUrlForLayer",
        "ecr:BatchGetImage",
        "ecr:BatchCheckLayerAvailability"
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

2. **Your account** has IAM permissions to call `ecr:GetAuthorizationToken` and the pull actions on the remote repository.

---

## For EKS to Pull from Remote ECR

If your EKS nodes need to pull directly from the remote ECR, you need to:

1. **Create an ECR pull secret:**
```powershell
# Get the auth token
$TOKEN = aws ecr get-login-password --region <REMOTE_REGION>

# Create Kubernetes secret
kubectl create secret docker-registry remote-ecr-secret `
  --docker-server=<REMOTE_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.<REMOTE_REGION>.amazonaws.com `
  --docker-username=AWS `
  --docker-password=$TOKEN
```

2. **Reference the secret in your deployment:**
```yaml
spec:
  imagePullSecrets:
    - name: remote-ecr-secret
  containers:
    - name: myapp
      image: <REMOTE_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.<REMOTE_REGION>.amazonaws.com/repo:tag
```

---

Would you like me to help you with specific values for the remote repository, or set up the cross-account permissions?

jpitzen: should I change the AWS context to the remote repository AWS context using its secrets?

GitHub Copilot: **It depends on your scenario:**

## Option 1: Stay in Your AWS Context (Recommended)

If the remote repository has granted **cross-account access** to your account (995553364920), you **don't need to switch contexts**. You can authenticate to the remote ECR from your current AWS credentials:

```powershell
# Your current AWS context stays the same
# Just authenticate to the remote ECR registry
aws ecr get-login-password --region <REMOTE_REGION> | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin <REMOTE_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.<REMOTE_REGION>.amazonaws.com
```

This works if:
- The remote account added an ECR repository policy allowing your account to pull
- Your IAM user/role has `ecr:GetAuthorizationToken` permission

---

## Option 2: Switch AWS Context (Use Remote Credentials)

You **should switch contexts** if:
- You don't have cross-account access set up
- You have credentials (access keys) for the remote account
- You're doing a one-time image copy

**To switch:**
```powershell
# Option A: Use a named profile
aws configure --profile remote-account
# Enter the remote account's Access Key ID and Secret Access Key

# Then use that profile
aws ecr get-login-password --region <REGION> --profile remote-account | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin <REMOTE_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.<REGION>.amazonaws.com
```

**Or temporarily set environment variables:**
```powershell
$env:AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID = "<REMOTE_ACCESS_KEY>"
$env:AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY = "<REMOTE_SECRET_KEY>"
$env:AWS_DEFAULT_REGION = "<REMOTE_REGION>"

# Now pull the image
aws ecr get-login-password | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin <REMOTE_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.<REGION>.amazonaws.com
docker pull <REMOTE_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.<REGION>.amazonaws.com/repo:tag

# Clear when done
Remove-Item Env:AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID
Remove-Item Env:AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY
```

---

## My Recommendation

1. **First try without switching** - run the `aws ecr get-login-password` command targeting the remote registry. If it works, cross-account is already configured.

2. **If that fails**, use `--profile` to add the remote credentials as a separate profile, so you can easily switch back and forth without losing your main context.

What's the remote account/repository you're trying to access? I can help you determine the best approach.

jpitzen: An error occurred (UnrecognizedClientException) when calling the GetAuthorizationToken operation: The security token included in the request is invalid.
error: cannot perform an interactive login from a non TTY device

GitHub Copilot: This error means your AWS credentials are invalid or expired for the remote account. Let's fix this:

## Solution: Configure a Named Profile for the Remote Account

Run these commands to set up the remote credentials properly: 

Ran terminal command: aws configure --profile remote-ecr

No problem. Here's the manual approach:

## Step-by-Step Fix

### 1. Create the Profile Manually

Create/edit the AWS credentials file. In PowerShell:

```powershell
notepad $HOME\.aws\credentials
```

Add these lines (replace with the remote account's actual keys):

```ini
[remote-ecr]
aws_access_key_id = YOUR_REMOTE_ACCESS_KEY
aws_secret_access_key = YOUR_REMOTE_SECRET_KEY
```

### 2. Authenticate to Remote ECR Using the Profile

```powershell
# Get password and store it in a variable first (avoids TTY issue)
$ECR_PASSWORD = aws ecr get-login-password --region <REMOTE_REGION> --profile remote-ecr

# Then login to Docker
docker login --username AWS --password $ECR_PASSWORD <REMOTE_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.<REMOTE_REGION>.amazonaws.com
```

### 3. Pull the Image

```powershell
docker pull <REMOTE_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.<REMOTE_REGION>.amazonaws.com/<REPO_NAME>:<TAG>
```

---

## Alternative: Use Environment Variables (No Profile)

If you don't want to save the credentials to a file:

```powershell
# Set credentials temporarily
$env:AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID = "REMOTE_ACCESS_KEY_HERE"
$env:AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY = "REMOTE_SECRET_KEY_HERE"
$env:AWS_DEFAULT_REGION = "REMOTE_REGION_HERE"

# Get ECR password
$ECR_PASSWORD = aws ecr get-login-password

# Login to Docker
docker login --username AWS --password $ECR_PASSWORD <REMOTE_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.<REMOTE_REGION>.amazonaws.com

# Pull your image
docker pull <REMOTE_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.<REMOTE_REGION>.amazonaws.com/<REPO>:<TAG>

# Clean up when done
Remove-Item Env:AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID
Remove-Item Env:AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY
Remove-Item Env:AWS_DEFAULT_REGION
```

---

**Key fix for the TTY error**: Store the password in a variable (`$ECR_PASSWORD`) first, then pass it to `docker login` with `--password` instead of piping.

jpitzen: login succeeded, I'd like to list the images there

GitHub Copilot: Great! To list the repositories and images in the remote ECR:

### 1. List All Repositories

```cmd
aws ecr describe-repositories --profile remote-ecr --region <REMOTE_REGION>
```

### 2. List Images in a Specific Repository

```cmd
aws ecr describe-images --repository-name <REPO_NAME> --profile remote-ecr --region <REMOTE_REGION>
```

### 3. List Just Image Tags (cleaner output)

```cmd
aws ecr list-images --repository-name <REPO_NAME> --profile remote-ecr --region <REMOTE_REGION>
```

---

### PowerShell Version (formatted output):

```powershell
# List all repos
aws ecr describe-repositories --profile remote-ecr --region <REMOTE_REGION> --query "repositories[].repositoryName" --output table

# List images with tags in a repo
aws ecr describe-images --repository-name <REPO_NAME> --profile remote-ecr --region <REMOTE_REGION> --query "imageDetails[].imageTags" --output table
```

Run the `describe-repositories` command first to see what repositories are available.

jpitzen: fix this cmd for windows cmdline

GitHub Copilot: For Windows CMD, remove the `--query` and `--output` options or use them with double quotes:

### List All Repositories

```cmd
aws ecr describe-repositories --profile remote-ecr --region <REMOTE_REGION>
```

### List Images in a Repository

```cmd
aws ecr list-images --repository-name <REPO_NAME> --profile remote-ecr --region <REMOTE_REGION>
```

### With Query (Windows CMD requires double quotes)

```cmd
aws ecr describe-repositories --profile remote-ecr --region <REMOTE_REGION> --query "repositories[].repositoryName" --output table
```

```cmd
aws ecr describe-images --repository-name <REPO_NAME> --profile remote-ecr --region <REMOTE_REGION> --query "imageDetails[].imageTags" --output table
```

The key difference: Windows CMD uses `"double quotes"` while PowerShell can use either. Start with the simple commands (no `--query`) to see the full output.

GitHub Copilot: For Windows CMD, you need to escape the JSON differently. Use double quotes and escape the inner quotes:

```cmd
aws ssm start-session --target i-05f0a52e52bddf73b --document-name AWS-StartPortForwardingSessionToRemoteHost --parameters "{\"host\":[\"usw-zlps-msexpsql-01.c5s06occm2dn.us-west-1.rds.amazonaws.com\"],\"portNumber\":[\"1433\"],\"localPortNumber\":[\"1433\"]}"
```

Or use a JSON file approach:

### Alternative: Use a Parameters File

1. Create a file called ssm-params.json:
```json
{
  "host": ["usw-zlps-msexpsql-01.c5s06occm2dn.us-west-1.rds.amazonaws.com"],
  "portNumber": ["1433"],
  "localPortNumber": ["1433"]
}
```

2. Run:
```cmd
aws ssm start-session --target i-05f0a52e52bddf73b --document-name AWS-StartPortForwardingSessionToRemoteHost --parameters file://ssm-params.json
```

jpitzen: sqlcmd to delete zldb

GitHub Copilot: Here's the `sqlcmd` command to delete the `zldb` database:

```cmd
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zldbadmin -P YourPassword -Q "DROP DATABASE zldb;"
```

Or if you want to be safer (check connections first and force drop):

```cmd
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zldbadmin -P YourPassword -Q "ALTER DATABASE zldb SET SINGLE_USER WITH ROLLBACK IMMEDIATE; DROP DATABASE zldb;"
```

**Replace `YourPassword`** with your actual `zldbadmin` password.

---

**Note:** Make sure your SSM port forwarding session is active on port 1433 before running this.

```

Or for a cleaner output:

```cmd
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zldbadmin -P YourPassword -Q "SELECT name FROM sys.databases ORDER BY name;"
```

**Replace `YourPassword`** with your actual password.

jpitzen: use sqlcmd to set a user as dbo in zldb

GitHub Copilot: ```cmd
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zldbadmin -P YourPassword -Q "USE zldb; ALTER ROLE db_owner ADD MEMBER [YourUserName];"
```

Or if you need to set the user as the actual database owner:

```cmd
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zldbadmin -P YourPassword -Q "ALTER AUTHORIZATION ON DATABASE::zldb TO [YourUserName];"
```

---

**For a specific user like `zldbuser`:**

```cmd
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zldbadmin -P YourPassword -Q "USE zldb; ALTER ROLE db_owner ADD MEMBER zldbuser;"
```

**Replace:**
- `YourPassword` with the actual password
- `YourUserName` or `zldbuser` with the actual username you want to grant dbo rights to

jpitzen: Changed database context to 'ZLDB'.
Msg 15151, Level 16, State 1, Server EC2AMAZ-U1GLV94, Line 1
Cannot add the principal 'pfuser', because it does not exist or you do not have permission.

GitHub Copilot: The user `pfuser` doesn't exist as a database user in `ZLDB`. You need to create it first.

### Step 1: Create the Login (if not exists)

```cmd
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zldbadmin -P YourPassword -Q "IF NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM sys.server_principals WHERE name = 'pfuser') CREATE LOGIN pfuser WITH PASSWORD = 'PfUserPassword';"
```

### Step 2: Create the Database User in ZLDB

```cmd
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zldbadmin -P YourPassword -Q "USE ZLDB; CREATE USER pfuser FOR LOGIN pfuser;"
```

### Step 3: Add to db_owner Role

```cmd
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zldbadmin -P YourPassword -Q "USE ZLDB; ALTER ROLE db_owner ADD MEMBER pfuser;"
```

---

**Or all in one command:**

```cmd
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zldbadmin -P YourPassword -Q "IF NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM sys.server_principals WHERE name = 'pfuser') CREATE LOGIN pfuser WITH PASSWORD = 'PfUserPassword'; USE ZLDB; IF NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM sys.database_principals WHERE name = 'pfuser') CREATE USER pfuser FOR LOGIN pfuser; ALTER ROLE db_owner ADD MEMBER pfuser;"
```

**Replace `PfUserPassword`** with the actual password you want for `pfuser`.

```

Or for more details:

```cmd

```

- `S` = SQL login
- `U` = Windows login
- `G` = Windows group

jpitzen: set up a docker push from local to the AWS repository

GitHub Copilot: Here are the steps to push a local Docker image to your AWS ECR repository:

### 1. Authenticate Docker to Your ECR

```cmd
aws ecr get-login-password --region us-west-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 995553364920.dkr.ecr.us-west-1.amazonaws.com
```

**For Windows CMD (if pipe doesn't work):**
```cmd
FOR /F "tokens=*" %i IN ('aws ecr get-login-password --region us-west-1') DO SET ECR_PASSWORD=%i
docker login --username AWS --password %ECR_PASSWORD% 995553364920.dkr.ecr.us-west-1.amazonaws.com
```

### 2. Tag Your Local Image

```cmd
docker tag <LOCAL_IMAGE_NAME>:<TAG> 995553364920.dkr.ecr.us-west-1.amazonaws.com/uw1-zlps-ecr-01:<TAG>
```

**Example:**
```cmd
docker tag zlui:latest 995553364920.dkr.ecr.us-west-1.amazonaws.com/uw1-zlps-ecr-01:zlui-latest
docker tag zlserver:1.0 995553364920.dkr.ecr.us-west-1.amazonaws.com/uw1-zlps-ecr-01:zlserver-1.0
```

### 3. Push to ECR

```cmd
docker push 995553364920.dkr.ecr.us-west-1.amazonaws.com/uw1-zlps-ecr-01:<TAG>
```

**Example:**
```cmd
docker push 995553364920.dkr.ecr.us-west-1.amazonaws.com/uw1-zlps-ecr-01:zlui-latest
```

---

### List Local Images First

```cmd
docker images
```

### Verify Push Succeeded

```cmd
aws ecr describe-images --repository-name uw1-zlps-ecr-01 --region us-west-1 --query "imageDetails[].imageTags" --output table
```

---

What's the name of the local image you want to push?

jpitzen: C:\Projects\aws-zl\yaml>kubectl apply -f zlzookeeper-statefulset.yaml
The StatefulSet "zlzookeeper" is invalid: spec: Forbidden: updates to statefulset spec for fields other than 'replicas', 'ordinals', 'template', 'updateStrategy', 'revisionHistoryLimit', 'persistentVolumeClaimRetentionPolicy' and 'minReadySeconds' are forbidden


GitHub Copilot: You need to delete and recreate the StatefulSet because you're trying to change immutable fields. 

```powershell
kubectl delete statefulset zlzookeeper
kubectl apply -f zlzookeeper-statefulset.yaml
```

**Note:** This will delete the existing zookeeper pods. The PVCs will be retained (unless you have a delete retention policy), so data should persist.

If you want to keep the PVCs explicitly:

```powershell
kubectl delete statefulset zlzookeeper --cascade=orphan
kubectl apply -f zlzookeeper-statefulset.yaml
```

The `--cascade=orphan` option deletes the StatefulSet but leaves the pods and PVCs running, then the new StatefulSet will adopt them.

jpitzen: I need to use Base64 to encrypt the password for the pfuser, unless I can use an AWS secret within the config and secret yaml I have.

GitHub Copilot: You have two options:

## Option 1: Base64 Encode for Kubernetes Secret (Simple)

Base64 encode the password:

```powershell
[Convert]::ToBase64String([Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetBytes("YourPfUserPassword"))
```

Then use it in your `db-secret.yaml`:
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-secret
type: Opaque
data:
  password: <BASE64_ENCODED_PASSWORD>
```

---

## Option 2: Use AWS Secrets Manager (More Secure)

You can store secrets in AWS Secrets Manager and access them in EKS using the **AWS Secrets Store CSI Driver**.

### Check if you have the CSI driver installed:
```powershell
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | Select-String "secrets-store"
```

If installed, you can create a `SecretProviderClass` to pull secrets from AWS Secrets Manager:

```yaml
apiVersion: secrets-store.csi.x-k8s.io/v1
kind: SecretProviderClass
metadata:
  name: aws-db-secrets
spec:
  provider: aws
  parameters:
    objects: |
      - objectName: "zldb-pfuser-password"
        objectType: "secretsmanager"
  secretObjects:
    - secretName: db-secret
      type: Opaque
      data:
        - objectName: zldb-pfuser-password
          key: password
```

---

**Which approach do you prefer?** 

For quick setup, Option 1 (Base64) works. For production with better security, Option 2 (AWS Secrets Manager) is recommended.

Let me check what secret YAML you currently have: 

Searched for files matching `**/*secret*.yaml`, 1 match

Read [](file:///c%3A/Projects/aws-zl/yaml/old/db-secret.yaml)

You're using `stringData` which **doesn't require Base64** - Kubernetes encodes it automatically!

Just update the password in db-secret.yaml and apply:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-secret
  namespace: default
type: Opaque
stringData:
  DB_USERNAME: "pfuser"
  DB_PASSWORD: "YourActualPassword"
```

Then apply:
```powershell
kubectl apply -f db-secret.yaml
```

If you want to use `data:` instead (pre-encoded), here's the Base64 command:

```powershell
[Convert]::ToBase64String([Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetBytes("YourActualPassword"))


jpitzen: can I use Base64 from the windows command line?

GitHub Copilot: Yes! In Windows CMD:

```cmd
powershell -Command "[Convert]::ToBase64String([Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetBytes('YourPassword'))"
```

Or using `certutil`:

```cmd
echo|set /p="YourPassword" > temp.txt && certutil -encode temp.txt encoded.txt && type encoded.txt && del temp.txt encoded.txt
```

---

**Simpler alternative** - use PowerShell directly:

```powershell
[Convert]::ToBase64String([Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetBytes("YourPassword"))
```

---

**Example:**
```cmd
powershell -Command "[Convert]::ToBase64String([Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetBytes('LJZ-wkVv@t_!d*PiM2tbx3TZ4h'))"
```

Output would be something like: `TEpaLXdrVnZAdF8hZCpQaU0ydGJ4M1RaNGg=`

jpitzen: I need a command to verify the zldb on the defined db instance

GitHub Copilot: You can use `sqlcmd` through SSM port forwarding. First, make sure your SSM session is running, then:

**Check if ZLDB exists and list tables:**

```cmd

```

**Or just verify connection to ZLDB:**

```cmd
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U pfuser -P YourPassword -Q "SELECT name FROM sys.databases WHERE name='ZLDB';"
```

**Check table count:**

```cmd
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U pfuser -P YourPassword -d ZLDB -Q "SELECT COUNT(*) AS TableCount FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES WHERE TABLE_TYPE='BASE TABLE';"
```

**The problem:** The RDS and EKS are in **different VPCs**:
- **RDS VPC:** `vpc-0699a7238e4e53bd8`
- **EKS VPC:** `vpc-07644cdd0224da4f3`

**Solutions:**

### Option 1: VPC Peering (Recommended)
Create a VPC peering connection between the two VPCs and update route tables.

### Option 2: Move RDS to EKS VPC
Create a new RDS instance in the EKS VPC or modify the existing one's subnet group.

### Option 3: Use the other RDS instance

GitHub Copilot: PowerShell escaping issue. Try this format:

```powershell
aws ssm start-session --target i-0db8baa2cdb746d4c --document-name AWS-StartPortForwardingSessionToRemoteHost --parameters '{\"host\":[\"usw1-zlps-msexpsql-01-eks.c5s06occm2dn.us-west-1.rds.amazonaws.com\"],\"portNumber\":[\"1433\"],\"localPortNumber\":[\"1433\"]}' --region us-west-1
```

Or use a file:

1. Create `ssm-params-eks.json`:
```json
{

I need the command to force assign pfuser as dbowner of zldb

GitHub Copilot: Summarized conversation historyHere's the SQL command to force assign `pfuser` as the database owner of `ZLDB`:

```sql
ALTER AUTHORIZATION ON DATABASE::ZLDB TO pfuser;
```

You can run this via sqlcmd through your SSM tunnel:

```powershell
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zlpsdbadmin -P "YourPassword" -Q "ALTER AUTHORIZATION ON DATABASE::ZLDB TO pfuser;"
```

Or if `pfuser` is not a login (only a database user), you'll need to use `sp_changedbowner` from within the database:

```sql
USE ZLDB;
EXEC sp_changedbowner 'pfuser';
```

Which approach would you like to use?

GitHub Copilot: The `pfuser` login doesn't exist at the server level. You need to create the login first, then the database user. Run these commands:

```powershell
# 1. Create the server login
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zlpsdbadmin -P "L7noGrgUFmkuvR6HJ*.VR_E" -Q "CREATE LOGIN pfuser WITH PASSWORD = 'YourPfuserPassword';"

# 2. Create the database user mapped to the login
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zlpsdbadmin -P "L7noGrgUFmkuvR6HJ*.VR_E" -d ZLDB -Q "CREATE USER pfuser FOR LOGIN pfuser;"

# 3. Now change the database owner
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zlpsdbadmin -P "L7noGrgUFmkuvR6HJ*.VR_E" -d ZLDB -Q "EXEC sp_changedbowner 'pfuser';"
```

Replace `'YourPfuserPassword'` with the actual password you want for pfuser. What password would you like to use for the pfuser account?

GitHub Copilot: The `zlpsdbadmin` user doesn't have permission to create logins. This is because on AWS RDS SQL Server, the master user has limited permissions. Let me check what's in the `crDBUser.sql` script and help you work around this: 

Read [](file:///c%3A/Projects/aws-zl/zldbcreate/crDBUser.sql)

The issue is that on **AWS RDS SQL Server**, you need to use `rds_set_configuration` stored procedures for certain operations. First, you need to create the LOGIN at the server level.

Try these commands in order:

```powershell
# 1. Create the LOGIN (on RDS, zlpsdbadmin should be able to do this)
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zlpsdbadmin -P "L7noGrgUFmkuvR6HJ*.VR_E" -Q "CREATE LOGIN pfuser WITH PASSWORD = 'PfUser@Pass123!';"

# 2. Switch to ZLDB and create the user
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zlpsdbadmin -P "L7noGrgUFmkuvR6HJ*.VR_E" -d ZLDB -Q "CREATE USER pfuser FOR LOGIN pfuser;"

# 3. Add to db_owner role
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zlpsdbadmin -P "L7noGrgUFmkuvR6HJ*.VR_E" -d ZLDB -Q "ALTER ROLE db_owner ADD MEMBER pfuser;"

# 4. Create schema
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zlpsdbadmin -P "L7noGrgUFmkuvR6HJ*.VR_E" -d ZLDB -Q "CREATE SCHEMA pfuser AUTHORIZATION pfuser;"

# 5. Set default schema
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zlpsdbadmin -P "L7noGrgUFmkuvR6HJ*.VR_E" -d ZLDB -Q "ALTER USER pfuser WITH DEFAULT_SCHEMA = pfuser;"
```

If step 1 fails with permission error, let me know and we'll need to check if `zlpsdbadmin` is the RDS master user. You can verify with:

```powershell
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zlpsdbadmin -P "L7noGrgUFmkuvR6HJ*.VR_E" -Q "SELECT name, type_desc FROM sys.server_principals WHERE name = 'zlpsdbadmin';"
```

jpitzen: should these run as the db owner?

GitHub Copilot: Yes, it's best practice to run schema creation scripts as the database owner (`pfuser`). Since pfuser is `db_owner` and has its own schema, running the scripts as pfuser ensures:

1. Objects are created with correct ownership
2. Schema-qualified objects belong to the right user
3. No permission issues later when the app connects as pfuser

Do you know the password for `pfuser`? If not, you can reset it:

```powershell
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U zlpsdbadmin -P "L7noGrgUFmkuvR6HJ*.VR_E" -Q "ALTER LOGIN pfuser WITH PASSWORD = 'NewPassword123!';"
```

Then run the scripts as pfuser:

```powershell

The issue is a **missing configuration file**. The error shows:

```
/opt/ZipLip/zlserver/WEB-INF/config/app/db/@SmDsDB@/pmDb@SmDsDBSuffix@.cfg (No such file or directory)
```

The `@SmDsDB@` and `@SmDsDBSuffix@` are **placeholders** that should be replaced with actual values. This suggests the application needs environment variables like `SmDsDB` and `SmDsDBSuffix` to locate the correct database config file.

This is likely a configuration issue within the application. The environment variables for the database connection might not be set up the way the application expects. The application is looking for config files based on these tokens.

What database type/configuration does the zlserver expect? You may need to set additional environment variables like `SmDsDB=mssql` or similar. Do you have documentation on what environment variables zlserver needs, or do you know what values `SmDsDB` and `SmDsDBSuffix` should be?

## Configuration Review

### ✅ **What's Configured Correctly:**

1. **zlapp-config.yaml** - ZooKeeper servers are properly configured with full DNS names:
   - `zlzookeeper-0.zk-hs.default.svc.cluster.local:2181,...`

2. **db-config.yaml** - Points to correct RDS instance:
   - `usw1-zlps-msexpsql-01-eks.c5s06occm2dn.us-west-1.rds.amazonaws.com`

3. **zk-cs.yaml** - Client service for ZooKeeper on port 2181 ✅

4. **zk-hs.yaml** - Headless service for ZooKeeper StatefulSet ✅

5. **zltika-service.yaml** - Service exposing port 9972 ✅

6. **zlserver-deployment.yaml** - Uses `envFrom` to load db-config, zlapp-config, db-secret ✅

7. **zlui-deployment.yaml** - Uses `envFrom` to load zlapp-config, db-config, db-secret ✅

### ❌ **What's Missing:**

1. **zlapp-config.yaml** - **Missing TIKA_HOST setting**
   - The zlserver needs to know where to find zltika service
   - Should add: `TIKA_HOST: "zltika"` or `__tika.ZLTikaService.Host: "zltika"`

2. **zltika-deployment.yaml** - **Missing envFrom** 
   - Unlike zlserver/zlui, zltika doesn't load from configmaps
   - May need `envFrom` for zlapp-config if it needs ZooKeeper connectivity

3. **zk-hs.yaml** - Port mismatch:
   - Has ports `2881` and `3881`
   - But zlzookeeper-statefulset.yaml containers use `2888` (peer) and `3888` (election)
   - Should be `2888` and `3888` to match

### Summary:
The main connectivity issue is that **zlserver doesn't know the tika host**. The `zlapp-config` needs a `TIKA_HOST` or equivalent environment variable set to `zltika` so zlserver can connect to the zltika service.

The environment variables are there, but the application's config system doesn't read from environment variables for these settings - it reads from the XML sheets which get persisted to ZooKeeper.

jpitzen: When researching, do not change the application, present your findings.

GitHub Copilot: Based on my research, here are the findings:

## Issue Analysis

**Problem**: The URL `/ps/app/null/PmApp/zlp_dummy` contains `null` where an application instance name should be.

## Root Cause

1. **ZooKeeper is empty** - The `/zltc` node doesn't exist in ZooKeeper yet. This is where the application stores its configuration, including the instance name.

2. **Fresh Installation State** - The `welcome.jsp` checks `ZLServer.canBootstrap()` which returns `true` for a fresh install. The welcome page is supposed to bootstrap the system and create the initial configuration in ZooKeeper.

3. **URL Construction Issue** - The application constructs URLs using a pattern like:
   ```
   /ps/app/{instanceName}/PmApp/...
   ```
   Since no instance name is configured yet, it's being rendered as `null`.

## Configuration Files Examined

| File | Purpose | Current State |
|------|---------|---------------|
| `pmappURL.cfg` | URL prefixes | `com.ziplip.prefix.appname=/ps` |
| `pmapp.cfg` | Main config | Points to `zkQuorum.cfg` for ZooKeeper |
| `zkQuorum.cfg` | ZK connection | Properly configured with 3 nodes |
| `Constants.cfg` | System constants | Standard values |

## The Problem Flow

1. User accesses `/app/home.jsp`
2. `home.jsp` detects fresh install → forwards to `/zlui/app/install/welcome.jsp`
3. `welcome.jsp` tries to load JS/CSS resources via `/ps/PmApp/jsCall/...`
4. These requests need the instance context which is `null`
5. Result: 404 error for `/ps/app/null/PmApp/zlp_dummy`

## Possible Solutions

1. **Direct JSP Access** - Try accessing the welcome page directly without going through the servlet routing:
   ```
   http://<elb>/zlui/app/install/welcome.jsp
   ```

2. **Bootstrap via API** - There may be a REST API or command-line tool to bootstrap the initial configuration without the UI.

3. **Pre-seed ZooKeeper** - Manually create the `/zltc` node structure in ZooKeeper with an initial instance configuration before accessing the UI.

4. **Check for Setup Scripts** - Look for installation scripts that may need to run first to initialize ZooKeeper.

## Recommendation

Try accessing the welcome page directly at:
```
http://k8s-default-zluiingr-e9951b35c2-755748288.us-west-1.elb.amazonaws.com/zlui/app/install/welcome.jsp
```

This bypasses the PmApp servlet routing that's causing the `null` instance name issue.

Ran terminal command: kubectl exec deployment/zlserver -- sh -c "tail -200 /usr/local/tomcat/logs/catalina.*.log 2>/dev/null"

Based on my research, here are the findings:

## Research Findings - "Access denied" Error

### Issue Description
When accessing `/zlui/app/install/welcome.jsp` directly, the `ZLJspFilter` at line 87 throws an "Access denied" `ServletException`.

### Root Cause Analysis

The `ZLJspFilter` is a security filter that applies to JSP, XML, HTML, and CFG files (as defined in `web.xml`):
```xml
<filter-mapping>
    <filter-name>ZLJSPFilter</filter-name>
    <url-pattern>*.jsp</url-pattern>
    <url-pattern>*.xml</url-pattern>
    <url-pattern>*.html</url-pattern>
    <url-pattern>*.cfg</url-pattern>
</filter-mapping>
```

The filter blocks direct access to JSP files as a security measure. This is designed to prevent users from directly accessing JSPs without going through the proper servlet (PmApp) routing.

### The Chicken-and-Egg Problem

1. **Going through PmApp servlet** (e.g., `/app/home.jsp`) → Redirects to welcome.jsp but fails with 404 because the instance name is `null` (not configured in ZooKeeper yet)

2. **Direct JSP access** (e.g., `/zlui/app/install/welcome.jsp`) → Blocked by `ZLJspFilter` with "Access denied"

### Key Configuration Points

| Setting | Location | Current Value |
|---------|----------|---------------|
| `/zltc` node | ZooKeeper | **Does not exist** |
| Instance Name | ZooKeeper (`/zltc`) | Not configured |
| `ZLJspFilter` | web.xml | Blocks direct JSP access |
| App Context | pmappURL.cfg | `/ps` |

### Potential Solutions

1. **Bootstrap via ZooKeeper** - The application may need initial ZooKeeper nodes created manually before the UI setup can proceed

2. **Check for CLI bootstrap tool** - There may be a command-line tool in `/opt/ZipLip/bin/` to initialize the system

3. **Modify filter behavior** - The filter may have an allowlist for installation/setup pages that isn't triggering correctly

4. **Check application documentation** - The ZL application likely has specific installation prerequisites or a setup script that needs to run before the web UI setup wizard works

### Recommended Next Steps

1. Check if there's a setup script in the application bin directory
2. Look for installation documentation for pre-requisites
3. Investigate if there's a way to seed the initial ZooKeeper configuration

jpitzen: list the changes that enabled tika properly

GitHub Copilot: Here are the changes that enabled Tika properly:

## Changes to Enable Tika Connectivity

### 1. Created `docconvert-configmap.yaml`
A new ConfigMap containing a modified `DocumentConversion.xml` file where the Tika host was changed from `localhost` to `zltika`:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: docconvert-config
data:
  DocumentConversion.xml: |
    # Contains __tika.ZLTikaService.Host=zltika
    # Instead of __tika.ZLTikaService.Host=localhost
```

### 2. Updated `zlserver-deployment.yaml`
Added a volume mount to override the default `DocumentConversion.xml` with the ConfigMap version:

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
  - name: zlserver
    volumeMounts:
    - name: docconvert-config
      mountPath: /opt/ZipLip/zlserver/WEB-INF/config/app/sheet/DocumentConversion.xml
      subPath: DocumentConversion.xml
  volumes:
  - name: docconvert-config
    configMap:
      name: docconvert-config
```

### 3. Updated `zlapp-config.yaml` ConfigMap
Added Tika-related environment variables:

```yaml
data:
  TIKA_HOST: "zltika"
  TIKA_PORT: "9972"
  __tika.ZLTikaService.Host: "zltika"
  __tika.ZLTikaService.Port: "9972"
```

### 4. Updated `zltika-deployment.yaml`
Added `envFrom` to load the `zlapp-config` ConfigMap:

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
  - name: zltika
    envFrom:
    - configMapRef:
        name: zlapp-config
```

---

**Root Cause**: The zlserver application was reading Tika configuration from `DocumentConversion.xml` (stored in ZooKeeper sheets), which had `__tika.ZLTikaService.Host=localhost` hardcoded. Environment variables alone weren't enough because the application loads this config from the XML file, not from environment variables.

**Solution**: Mount a ConfigMap with the corrected XML file directly into the container, overriding the default configuration.

jpitzen: how can I create a folder under the /var/ZipLip/Vault/ location?

GitHub Copilot: You can create a folder under `/var/ZipLip/Vault/` by executing a command in one of the pods that has it mounted (zlui): 

Ran terminal command: kubectl exec deployment/zlui -- mkdir -p /var/ZipLip/Vault/test-folder

Ran terminal command: kubectl exec deployment/zlui -- ls -la /var/ZipLip/Vault/

Done! The folder `test-folder` was created. 

**Options to create folders:**

1. **From a pod** (as shown above):
   ```powershell
   kubectl exec deployment/zlui -- mkdir -p /var/ZipLip/Vault/your-folder-name
   ```

2. **Interactive shell**:
   ```powershell
   kubectl exec -it deployment/zlui -- /bin/bash
   # Then: mkdir -p /var/ZipLip/Vault/your-folder-name
   ```

3. **Create multiple folders**:
   ```powershell
   kubectl exec deployment/zlui -- mkdir -p /var/ZipLip/Vault/folder1 /var/ZipLip/Vault/folder2
   ```

jpitzen: excellent... can the applicaiton gateway use a friendlier name?

GitHub Copilot: Yes! You can create a friendlier DNS name for your Application Load Balancer. You have a few options:

**Option 1: Use Route 53 (if you have a domain)**
Create a CNAME or Alias record pointing to the ALB:
```
zlapp.yourdomain.com → k8s-default-zluiingr-e9951b35c2-755748288.us-west-1.elb.amazonaws.com
```

**Option 2: Add a custom hostname to the Ingress**
You can configure the Ingress to respond to a specific hostname.

Let me check your current ingress configuration: 

Ran terminal command: kubectl get ingress -o yaml

**Do you have a domain name you'd like to use?** 

If so, I can help you:
1. **Route 53** - Create an Alias record pointing to the ALB (recommended for AWS)
2. **External DNS provider** - Create a CNAME record

For example, if you have a domain like `example.com`, you could set up:
- `zlapp.example.com`
- `zl.example.com`
- `archive.example.com`

What domain would you like to use? Or if you don't have one, you could:
- **Register a new domain** via Route 53 (~$12/year for .com)
- **Use a free subdomain service** (less professional)

jpitzen: let's wait

GitHub Copilot: Sounds good! The ALB URL will continue to work as-is:

**Current URL:** `http://k8s-default-zluiingr-e9951b35c2-755748288.us-west-1.elb.amazonaws.com`

jpitzen: can I just add that to my DNS server and create an alias to it for now?

GitHub Copilot: Yes, absolutely! Just create a **CNAME record** on your DNS server:

```
zlapp.yourdomain.com  CNAME  k8s-default-zluiingr-e9951b35c2-755748288.us-west-1.elb.amazonaws.com
```

Replace `zlapp.yourdomain.com` with whatever friendly name you want (e.g., `zl.company.com`, `archive.internal.com`, etc.).

That's all you need - no changes required on the AWS/Kubernetes side. The ALB will accept requests regardless of what hostname is used to reach it.

jpitzen: Explain the need for this step:
STEP 3: Get Cluster OIDC Issuer
-------------------------------
PURPOSE: Retrieve the OIDC issuer URL (needed for trust policies)
SHELL: All shells (PowerShell, Bash, CMD)

# All Shells
aws eks describe-cluster --name usw1-zlps-eks-01 --region us-west-1 --query "cluster.identity.oidc.issuer" --output text

OUTPUT: https://oidc.eks.us-west-1.amazonaws.com/id/C0CA93DEA5736D0F19AC427D5A0A123E


GitHub Copilot: ## Explanation of STEP 3: Get Cluster OIDC Issuer

**What is OIDC?**
OIDC (OpenID Connect) is an identity layer built on OAuth 2.0 that allows services to verify identity. Every EKS cluster has a unique OIDC issuer URL that serves as its identity provider.

**Why is this step needed?**

1. **IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA)** - This is the primary reason. The OIDC issuer URL is required to create a trust relationship between:
   - AWS IAM roles (which grant permissions to AWS resources like EBS, EFS, S3)
   - Kubernetes service accounts (which pods use to authenticate)

2. **Trust Policy Configuration** - The OIDC issuer ID (the `C0CA93DEA5736D0F19AC427D5A0A123E` part) is embedded in IAM trust policies to ensure only pods from *your specific cluster* can assume IAM roles. For example:

   ```json
   {
     "Effect": "Allow",
     "Principal": {
       "Federated": "arn:aws:iam::995553364920:oidc-provider/oidc.eks.us-west-1.amazonaws.com/id/C0CA93DEA5736D0F19AC427D5A0A123E"
     },
     "Action": "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity",
     "Condition": {
       "StringEquals": {
         "oidc.eks.us-west-1.amazonaws.com/id/C0CA93DEA5736D0F19AC427D5A0A123E:sub": "system:serviceaccount:kube-system:ebs-csi-controller-sa"
       }
     }
   }
   ```

3. **Used in subsequent steps** - This OIDC URL is needed for:
   - **STEP 4**: Creating the OIDC identity provider in IAM
   - **STEP 6-12**: Creating IAM roles for EBS CSI driver
   - **STEP 15-17**: Creating IAM roles for EFS CSI driver
   - Any CSI driver or add-on that needs AWS API access

**In simple terms**: Without the OIDC issuer, your Kubernetes pods cannot securely access AWS services (EBS volumes, EFS file systems, S3 buckets) because there's no way to establish trust between Kubernetes and AWS IAM.

jpitzen: Explain this step why is it needed and in what subsequent steps:

STEP 4: Get Cluster VPC Information
-----------------------------------
PURPOSE: Identify the VPC and subnets for additional resource configuration
SHELL: All shells (PowerShell, Bash, CMD)

# All Shells
aws eks describe-cluster --name usw1-zlps-eks-01 --query "cluster.resourcesVpcConfig.{VPC: vpcId, Subnets: subnetIds}" --output table

GitHub Copilot: ## Explanation of STEP 4: Get Cluster VPC Information

**What does this command retrieve?**
This command returns:
- **VPC ID**: `vpc-07644cdd0224da4f3` - The Virtual Private Cloud where your EKS cluster lives
- **Subnet IDs**: The network subnets (both public and private) that the cluster uses

**Why is this step needed?**

When eksctl creates a cluster, it either creates a new VPC or uses an existing one. You need to know the VPC and subnet IDs to configure additional AWS resources that must be in the **same network** as your EKS cluster.

**Subsequent steps that use this information:**

| Step | Resource | How VPC/Subnet Info is Used |
|------|----------|----------------------------|
| **STEP 19** | EFS Mount Targets | Mount targets must be created in the same subnets as your EKS nodes so pods can access EFS storage |
| **STEP 40** | ALB Ingress | The `alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/subnets` annotation specifies which subnets the load balancer uses: `subnet-01c24772a882a3a1b,subnet-06eb80d784d985109` |
| **STEP 40** | Helm install ALB Controller | `--set vpcId=vpc-07644cdd0224da4f3` tells the controller which VPC to manage |
| **Security Groups** | All resources | Security groups are VPC-scoped; you need the VPC ID to create/reference them |

**Example from your deployment:**

In **STEP 40** (ALB Controller installation):
```
helm install aws-load-balancer-controller ... --set vpcId=vpc-07644cdd0224da4f3
```

In **zlui-ingress.yaml**:
```yaml
annotations:
  alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/subnets: subnet-01c24772a882a3a1b,subnet-06eb80d784d985109
```

**In simple terms**: AWS resources need to be in the same "neighborhood" (VPC/subnets) to communicate with each other. This step tells you the address of that neighborhood so you can place EFS, load balancers, and other resources there.

jpitzen: format the response like the previous response describing step 3

GitHub Copilot: ## Explanation of STEP 4: Get Cluster VPC Information

**What is a VPC?**
A VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) is an isolated virtual network within AWS where your resources run. Every EKS cluster is deployed inside a VPC, and all associated resources (nodes, load balancers, storage) must be in the same or connected VPCs.

**Why is this step needed?**

1. **Network Consistency** - AWS resources must be deployed in the same VPC/subnets to communicate with each other. This command tells you the "network address" of your cluster so you can place additional resources there.

2. **Subnet Selection** - The output shows which subnets are available:
   - **Public subnets** (`subnet-01c24772a882a3a1b`, `subnet-06eb80d784d985109`) - for internet-facing resources like ALB
   - **Private subnets** (`subnet-0c861c54ffa4ddbe6`, `subnet-039bd7f435475c3dc`) - for internal resources

3. **Used in subsequent steps** - The VPC ID and subnet IDs are required for:

| Step | Resource | How VPC/Subnet Info is Used |
|------|----------|----------------------------|
| **STEP 19** | EFS Mount Targets | `--subnet-id subnet-01c24772a882a3a1b` - Mount targets must be in EKS node subnets |
| **STEP 40** | ALB Controller | `--set vpcId=vpc-07644cdd0224da4f3` - Controller needs to know which VPC to manage |
| **STEP 41** | Ingress YAML | `alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/subnets: subnet-01c24772a882a3a1b,subnet-06eb80d784d985109` |
| **Security Groups** | All resources | Security groups are VPC-scoped |

**Example from your deployment:**

In **STEP 19** (EFS Mount Targets):
```
aws efs create-mount-target --file-system-id fs-0a4ec87cd91178047 --subnet-id subnet-01c24772a882a3a1b ...
aws efs create-mount-target --file-system-id fs-0a4ec87cd91178047 --subnet-id subnet-06eb80d784d985109 ...
```

In **STEP 40** (ALB Controller Helm install):
```
helm install aws-load-balancer-controller ... --set vpcId=vpc-07644cdd0224da4f3
```

In **zlui-ingress.yaml**:
```yaml
annotations:
  alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/subnets: subnet-01c24772a882a3a1b,subnet-06eb80d784d985109
```

**In simple terms**: Without knowing the VPC and subnet IDs, you cannot create EFS mount points that your pods can access, nor can you deploy load balancers that route traffic to your application. This step gives you the "street address" of your cluster's neighborhood.

jpitzen: Describe this section:
3. PHASE 2: ECR SETUP AND IMAGE MANAGEMENT

GitHub Copilot: ## Explanation of PHASE 2: ECR SETUP AND IMAGE MANAGEMENT

**What is ECR?**
ECR (Elastic Container Registry) is AWS's private Docker image registry. It stores your container images securely and makes them available to your EKS cluster for deployment.

**Why is this phase needed?**

1. **Private Image Storage** - Your ZL application images (`zlserver`, `zltika`, `zlzookeeper`) are proprietary and stored in a private registry. EKS nodes need credentials to pull these images.

2. **Authentication Between EKS and ECR** - By default, Kubernetes pods cannot access private registries. This phase creates the necessary credentials (called "image pull secrets") that allow pods to authenticate with ECR.

3. **Token-Based Security** - ECR uses temporary authentication tokens (valid for 12 hours) generated by the AWS CLI, which is more secure than static passwords.

**Steps in this phase:**

| Step | Purpose | Used By |
|------|---------|---------|
| **STEP 5** | Verify ECR repository exists | Confirms `uw1-zlps-ecr-01` is accessible |
| **STEP 6** | List images in ECR | Shows available images: `zlserver-11.0.1-b123`, `zltika-11.0.1-b123`, `zlzookeeper-11.0.1-b123` |
| **STEP 7** | Create docker-secret in `kube-system` | Allows CSI driver pods to pull images |
| **STEP 8** | Create docker-secret in `default` | Allows application pods (zlui, zlserver, zltika, zlzookeeper) to pull images |

**How it connects to subsequent phases:**

The `docker-secret` created here is referenced in every deployment YAML:

```yaml
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      imagePullSecrets:
        - name: docker-secret    # ← References the secret from STEP 7/8
      containers:
        - name: zlui
          image: 995553364920.dkr.ecr.us-west-1.amazonaws.com/uw1-zlps-ecr-01:zlserver-11.0.1-b123
```

**Used in subsequent steps:**
- **STEP 32**: ZooKeeper StatefulSet pulls `zlzookeeper-11.0.1-b123`
- **STEP 35**: Tika Deployment pulls `zltika-11.0.1-b123`
- **STEP 36**: ZL Server Deployment pulls `zlserver-11.0.1-b123`
- **STEP 37**: ZL UI Deployment pulls `zlserver-11.0.1-b123`

**In simple terms**: Without this phase, your EKS cluster would fail to start any pods with `ImagePullBackOff` errors because it wouldn't have the credentials to download your container images from ECR. This is the "key to the warehouse" where your application images are stored.
