jpitzen: PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> # AWS Inspector - Fetch ECR Image Findings
# Generated: 2025-12-16T12:55:56.185Z
# Repository: uw1-zlps-ecr-01
# Target: zlserver-11.0.1-b123
# Region: us-west-1

# Output folder for scan results (same as Grype scans)
$vscanDir = "C:\\Projects\\aws-zl\\Deployment Guide\\vscan"

# Create vscan folder if it doesn't exist
if (!(Test-Path $vscanDir)) {
    New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path $vscanDir -Force | Out-Null
    Write-Host "Created vscan folder: $vscanDir" -ForegroundColor Green
}

# Generate timestamp for filename
$timestamp = Get-Date -Format "yyyyMMddHHmmss"
$outputFile = Join-Path $vscanDir "InspectorScan_$timestamp.json"
$manifestFile = Join-Path $vscanDir "scans.json"

# Build the filter criteria JSON and save to temp file
$filterFile = Join-Path $env:TEMP "inspector-filter.json"
$filterJson = '{"ecrImageRepositoryName":[{"comparison":"EQUALS","value":"uw1-zlps-ecr-01"}],"ecrImageTags":[{"comparison":"EQUALS","value":"zlserver-11.0.1-b123"}]}'
[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($filterFile, $filterJson)

# Step 1: Fetch findings from AWS Inspector
Write-Host "Fetching AWS Inspector findings..." -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "Repository: uw1-zlps-ecr-01" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "Target: zlserver-11.0.1-b123" -ForegroundColor Yellow

# Set encoding and fetch - use pagination to get all results
$env:PYTHONIOENCODING = "utf-8"
[Console]::OutputEncoding = [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8

$allFindings = @()
$nextToken = $null
$page = 0

do {
    $page++
    Write-Host "Fetching page $page..." -ForegroundColor Gray
    
    if ($nextToken) {
        $result = aws inspector2 list-findings --region us-west-1 --filter-criteria file://$filterFile --starting-token $nextToken --output json 2>&1 | Out-String
    } else {
        $result = aws inspector2 list-findings --region us-west-1 --filter-criteria file://$filterFile --output json 2>&1 | Out-String
    }
    
    try {
        $json = $result | ConvertFrom-Json
        $allFindings += $json.findings
        $nextToken = $json.nextToken
    } catch {
        Write-Host "Warning: Error parsing page $page" -ForegroundColor Yellow
        $nextToken = $null
    }
} while ($nextToken -and $page -lt 20)

# Build final JSON and save
$finalJson = @{ findings = $allFindings } | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 20 -Compress
[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($outputFile, $finalJson, [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8)

# Clean up temp filter file  
Remove-Item $filterFile -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue

# Step 2: Display summary
Write-Host ""
Write-Host "Findings Summary:" -ForegroundColor Cyan
$findingCount = $allFindings.Count

if ($findingCount -gt 0) {
    $critical = ($allFindings | Where-Object { $_.severity -eq "CRITICAL" }).Count
    $high = ($allFindings | Where-Object { $_.severity -eq "HIGH" }).Count
    $medium = ($allFindings | Where-Object { $_.severity -eq "MEDIUM" }).Count
    $low = ($allFindings | Where-Object { $_.severity -eq "LOW" }).Count
    
    Write-Host "CRITICAL: $critical" -ForegroundColor Red
    Write-Host "HIGH:     $high" -ForegroundColor DarkYellow
    Write-Host "MEDIUM:   $medium" -ForegroundColor Yellow
    Write-Host "LOW:      $low" -ForegroundColor Gray
    Write-Host ""
    Write-Host "Total findings: $findingCount" -ForegroundColor Yellow
} else {
    Write-Host "No findings returned (image may not be scanned yet)" -ForegroundColor Yellow
}

# Step 3: Update scans manifest
Write-Host "Updating scan manifest..." -ForegroundColor Cyan
$grypeFiles = Get-ChildItem -Path $vscanDir -Filter "RawScanResults_*.json" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Name
$inspectorFiles = Get-ChildItem -Path $vscanDir -Filter "InspectorScan_*.json" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Name
$allFiles = @($grypeFiles) + @($inspectorFiles) | Sort-Object -Descending
$allFiles | ConvertTo-Json | Out-File -FilePath $manifestFile -Encoding UTF8
Write-Host "Manifest updated with $($allFiles.Count) scan(s)" -ForegroundColor Green

# Done
Write-Host ""
Write-Host "========================================" -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host "Inspector scan complete!" -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host "Results saved to: $outputFile" -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host "========================================" -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host ""
Write-Host "Click 'Refresh' in the scan dropdown to load results." -ForegroundColor Yellow
Fetching AWS Inspector findings...
Repository: uw1-zlps-ecr-01
Target: zlserver-11.0.1-b123
Fetching page 1...

Findings Summary:
No findings returned (image may not be scanned yet)
Updating scan manifest...
Manifest updated with 2 scan(s)

========================================
Inspector scan complete!
Results saved to: C:\\Projects\\aws-zl\\Deployment Guide\\vscan\InspectorScan_20251216075611.json
========================================

Click 'Refresh' in the scan dropdown to load results.

aws inspector2 list-findings --region us-west-1 --filter-criteria '{"ecrImageRepositoryName":[{"comparison":"EQUALS","value":"uw1-zlps-ecr-01"}]}' --query "findings[].resources[].details.awsEcrContainerImage.imageTags" --output json | ConvertFrom-Json | ForEach-Object { $_ } | Sort-Object -Unique | Select-Object -First 20

The newer images (`*-11.0.1-b123`) pushed on Dec 8 haven't been scanned by Inspector yet. This could be because:
1. Inspector scanning is still in progress
2. Inspector needs to be triggered manually for new images
3. There's a delay in Inspector's continuous scanning

**For now**, when selecting images for Inspector, you should pick from the `11.1.0-b1140` versions which have findings. The `11.0.1-b123` images show 0 findings because Inspector hasn't scanned them yet.

Now refresh and try the "Check Scan Status" button on `zlserver-11.0.1-b123`. It will show you the `UNSUPPORTED_MEDIA_TYPE` error and explain that the image needs to be rebuilt.

**Summary of findings:**
- The newer images (`*-11.0.1-b123`) have `UNSUPPORTED_MEDIA_TYPE` status - Inspector can't scan them
- The older images (`*11.1.0-b1140`) are `ACTIVE` and `SUCCESSFUL` - they have findings
- The fix is to rebuild the images with a newer Docker version or use `docker buildx --provenance=false`

For now, you can only get Inspector findings for the `11.1.0-b1140` images. The `11.0.1-b123` images need to be rebuilt.

Summarized conversation historyDone! I've added a comprehensive troubleshooting section with three options:

1. **Option 1: Rebuild with Provenance Disabled** - Best if you have the Dockerfile, uses `--provenance=false` flag

2. **Option 2: Re-tag and Push** - Pull existing image, re-tag with new version, push with standard `docker push` (not buildx)

3. **Option 3: Check Status** - Command to view all images and their scan status

The key insight is that Docker BuildKit/buildx adds attestation manifests by default, which Inspector sometimes can't parse. Disabling provenance or using the standard `docker push` creates a simpler manifest that Inspector can handle.

GitHub Copilot: Summarized conversation historyI'll walk you through the complete process to rebuild the images locally with the correct settings, push to ECR, and update the pods. Let me create a step-by-step guide.

## Step-by-Step: Rebuild Images for AWS Inspector Compatibility

### Step 1: Rebuild Images Locally (with `--provenance=false`)

First, navigate to your Docker build directory where your Dockerfiles are located. Then rebuild each image:

```powershell
# Navigate to your build directory (adjust path as needed)
cd C:\path\to\your\docker\builds

# Rebuild zlserver
docker buildx build --provenance=false --sbom=false -t zlserver-11.0.1-b123 -f Dockerfile.zlserver .

# Rebuild zltika
docker buildx build --provenance=false --sbom=false -t zltika-11.0.1-b123 -f Dockerfile.zltika .

# Rebuild zlzookeeper (if needed)
docker buildx build --provenance=false --sbom=false -t zlzookeeper-11.0.1-b123 -f Dockerfile.zlzookeeper .

# Rebuild zlfile (if needed)
docker buildx build --provenance=false --sbom=false -t zlfile-11.0.1-b123 -f Dockerfile.zlfile .
```

**Alternative:** If you don't use buildx, standard `docker build` works fine:
```powershell
docker build -t zlserver-11.0.1-b123 -f Dockerfile.zlserver .
```

---

### Step 2: Tag Images for ECR

Tag each image with your ECR repository URI:

```powershell
# Set your ECR variables
$AWS_ACCOUNT = "536697269866"
$AWS_REGION = "us-west-1"
$ECR_REPO = "uw1-zlps-ecr-01"

# Tag each image
docker tag zlserver-11.0.1-b123 "$AWS_ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$AWS_REGION.amazonaws.com/${ECR_REPO}:zlserver-11.0.1-b123"
docker tag zltika-11.0.1-b123 "$AWS_ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$AWS_REGION.amazonaws.com/${ECR_REPO}:zltika-11.0.1-b123"
docker tag zlzookeeper-11.0.1-b123 "$AWS_ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$AWS_REGION.amazonaws.com/${ECR_REPO}:zlzookeeper-11.0.1-b123"
docker tag zlfile-11.0.1-b123 "$AWS_ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$AWS_REGION.amazonaws.com/${ECR_REPO}:zlfile-11.0.1-b123"
```

---

### Step 3: Authenticate to ECR

```powershell
aws ecr get-login-password --region us-west-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 536697269866.dkr.ecr.us-west-1.amazonaws.com
```

---

### Step 4: Push Images to ECR

```powershell
$ECR_URI = "536697269866.dkr.ecr.us-west-1.amazonaws.com/uw1-zlps-ecr-01"

# Push each image
docker push "${ECR_URI}:zlserver-11.0.1-b123"
docker push "${ECR_URI}:zltika-11.0.1-b123"
docker push "${ECR_URI}:zlzookeeper-11.0.1-b123"
docker push "${ECR_URI}:zlfile-11.0.1-b123"
```

---

### Step 5: Verify Inspector Scan Status

Wait 1-2 minutes for Inspector to detect the new images, then check status:

```powershell
aws inspector2 list-coverage --region us-west-1 --filter-criteria '{"resourceType":[{"comparison":"EQUALS","value":"AWS_ECR_CONTAINER_IMAGE"}],"ecrRepositoryName":[{"comparison":"EQUALS","value":"uw1-zlps-ecr-01"}]}' --query "coveredResources[*].{tag:resourceMetadata.ecrImage.tags[0],status:scanStatus.statusCode,reason:scanStatus.reason}" --output table
```

You should see the status change from `INACTIVE/UNSUPPORTED_MEDIA_TYPE` to `ACTIVE/SUCCESSFUL`.

---

### Step 6: Update Kubernetes Pods

Force the pods to pull the updated images:

```powershell
# Option A: Rolling restart (zero-downtime)
kubectl rollout restart deployment/zlserver -n zlps
kubectl rollout restart deployment/zltika -n zlps
kubectl rollout restart statefulset/zlzookeeper -n zlps
kubectl rollout restart deployment/zlfile -n zlps

# Option B: Delete pods (they'll recreate with new image)
kubectl delete pod -l app=zlserver -n zlps
kubectl delete pod -l app=zltika -n zlps
kubectl delete pod -l app=zlzookeeper -n zlps
kubectl delete pod -l app=zlfile -n zlps
```

**Note:** If your pods use `imagePullPolicy: Always`, the rolling restart will pull the new image. If set to `IfNotPresent`, you may need to delete the pods.

---

### Step 7: Verify Pods Are Running New Images

```powershell
# Check pod status
kubectl get pods -n zlps

# Verify image digest (should be different from before)
kubectl get pods -n zlps -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.containerStatuses[0].imageID}{"\n"}{end}'
```

---

Would you like me to help fix the zookeeper configuration issue separately?

jpitzen: yes, this is critical

GitHub Copilot: Let me investigate the zookeeper configuration issue. First, I'll check the configmap and statefulset configuration: 

Ran terminal command: kubectl get configmap -n default | Select-String -Pattern "zook|zk"

Ran terminal command: kubectl get configmap -n default

Ran terminal command: kubectl get configmap zlapp-config -n default -o yaml

Ran terminal command: kubectl get statefulset zlzookeeper -n default -o yaml

Ran terminal command: kubectl get svc -n default | Select-String -Pattern "zk|zookeeper"

Ran terminal command: kubectl exec -it zlzookeeper-0 -n default -- cat /opt/ZipLip/ZLZooKeeper/config/zk.cfg 2>$null

Ran terminal command: kubectl exec -it zlzookeeper-0 -n default -- cat /opt/ZipLip/ZLZooKeeper/config/zkQuorum.cfg 2>$null

I see! The config uses ports `2881` and `3881` but the StatefulSet exposes ports `2888` and `3888`. Let me check the actual services: 

Ran terminal command: kubectl get svc zk-hs -n default -o yaml

Found the issue! There's a **port mismatch**:

| Component | Peer Port | Election Port |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| zkQuorum.cfg (config inside container) | **2881** | **3881** |
| StatefulSet containers | 2888 | 3888 |
| zk-hs Service | 2888 | 3888 |

The config file inside the container uses `2881/3881` but the Kubernetes services expose `2888/3888`. We need to update the zkQuorum.cfg to use the correct ports.

Let me create an updated configmap for this: 

Ran terminal command: kubectl exec -it zlzookeeper-0 -n default -- ls -la /opt/ZipLip/ZLZooKeeper/config/

The zkQuorum.cfg file was modified on Dec 8. This config file is baked into the image. We need to override it. Let me check if there's a way to mount a configmap: 

Ran terminal command: kubectl exec -it zlzookeeper-0 -n default -- cat /opt/ZipLip/ZLZooKeeper/config/zkChild.cfg 2>$null

The config is in the image. We have two options:

1. **Quick fix**: Create a ConfigMap and mount it to override zkQuorum.cfg
2. **Rebuild image**: Fix the ports in the image itself

Let's do the quick fix with a ConfigMap: 

Ran terminal command: @"
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: zk-quorum-config
  namespace: default
data:
  zkQuorum.cfg: |
    #if !_ZK_SERVER_CONFIG_FILE_IS_INCLUDED
    #define _ZK_SERVER_CONFIG_FILE_IS_INCLUDED = true

    //Replace below commented sample auth configuration with output from the command "zkauth -g" on the command line
    _zlzk.auth=#com.zlti.zlzookeeper.ZLZKAuth~~/aIpFRJV+AkUAxTpiXuKbxggADMgJwSSbqk6eDR1mu+hzrO/H923Q/jbLTrNqXnGVt+DmJkd7B0BqsMBzCJWCA==

    // id,machine,clientPort,peerPort,electionPort,dataDir,[hostname]
    zkQuorum.1=1~~zlzookeeper-0~~2181~~2888~~3888~~/var/ZipLip/DATA/ZooKeeper/zk1_2888~~zlzookeeper-0.zk-hs.default.svc.cluster.local
    zkQuorum.2=2~~zlzookeeper-1~~2181~~2888~~3888~~/var/ZipLip/DATA/ZooKeeper/zk2_2888~~zlzookeeper-1.zk-hs.default.svc.cluster.local
    zkQuorum.3=3~~zlzookeeper-2~~2181~~2888~~3888~~/var/ZipLip/DATA/ZooKeeper/zk3_2888~~zlzookeeper-2.zk-hs.default.svc.cluster.local

    acv.zkQuorum=#wsi.config.AllConfigVariables~~@NAMES_THAT_START_WITH@~~zkQuorum.

    #endif
"@ | kubectl apply -f -

Now let's update the StatefulSet to mount this ConfigMap: 

Ran terminal command: kubectl patch statefulset zlzookeeper -n default --type='json' -p='[
  {"op": "add", "path": "/spec/template/spec/volumes", "value": [{"name": "zk-quorum-config", "configMap": {"name": "zk-quorum-config"}}]},
  {"op": "add", "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/volumeMounts/-", "value": {"name": "zk-quorum-config", "mountPath": "/opt/ZipLip/ZLZooKeeper/config/zkQuorum.cfg", "subPath": "zkQuorum.cfg"}}
]'

Now delete the failing pods so they recreate with the new config: 

Ran terminal command: kubectl delete pod zlzookeeper-0 zlzookeeper-1 zlzookeeper-2 -n default --force --grace-period=0

Ran terminal command: Start-Sleep -Seconds 30; kubectl get pods -n default -l app=zlzookeeper -o wide

**All three zookeeper pods are now running!** Let me verify they're healthy and can communicate: 

Ran terminal command: kubectl logs zlzookeeper-0 -n default --tail=30

Excellent! The logs show:
- Leader election working
- Session authentication successful
- Cluster is communicating properly 

Ran terminal command: kubectl logs zlzookeeper-1 -n default --tail=15

Ran terminal command: kubectl logs zlzookeeper-2 -n default --tail=15

**The zookeeper cluster is fully operational!** Node 2 (zlzookeeper-2) is the leader, and nodes 0 and 1 are followers.

Now let's update the statefulset to use the new v2 image that Inspector can scan: 

Ran terminal command: kubectl set image statefulset/zlzookeeper zlzookeeper=995553364920.dkr.ecr.us-west-1.amazonaws.com/uw1-zlps-ecr-01:zlzookeeper-11.0.1-b123-v2 -n default

Ran terminal command: kubectl rollout status statefulset/zlzookeeper -n default --timeout=180s

Ran terminal command: kubectl get pods -n default | Select-String -Pattern "zl"

All pods are running. Let me verify the images: 

Ran terminal command: kubectl get pods -n default -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.spec.containers[0].image}{"\n"}{end}' | Select-String -Pattern "zl"

All pods are now running the `-v2` images (except zlui which uses a different image).

Let's verify zookeeper cluster health one more time: 

Ran terminal command: kubectl logs zlzookeeper-0 -n default --tail=10

Excellent! Everything is healthy. Let's do a final AWS Inspector scan status check: 

Ran terminal command: aws inspector2 list-coverage --region us-west-1 --filter-criteria '{"resourceType":[{"comparison":"EQUALS","value":"AWS_ECR_CONTAINER_IMAGE"}],"ecrRepositoryName":[{"comparison":"EQUALS","value":"uw1-zlps-ecr-01"}]}' --query "coveredResources[*].{tag:resourceMetadata.ecrImage.tags[0],status:scanStatus.statusCode,reason:scanStatus.reason}" --output table

$pinfo = New-Object System.Diagnostics.ProcessStartInfo
$pinfo.FileName = "aws"
$pinfo.Arguments = "inspector2 list-findings --region us-west-1 --filter-criteria file://$filterFile --output json"
$pinfo.RedirectStandardOutput = $true
$pinfo.RedirectStandardError = $true
$pinfo.UseShellExecute = $false
$pinfo.CreateNoWindow = $true
$p = New-Object System.Diagnostics.Process
$p.StartInfo = $pinfo
$p.Start() | Out-Null
$stdout = $p.StandardOutput.ReadToEnd()
$stderr = $p.StandardError.ReadToEnd()
$p.WaitForExit()

Write-Host "Output length: $($stdout.Length) chars"

$fi = Get-Item $tempJsonFile

jpitzen: will it consolidate the findings into one file when complete?

GitHub Copilot: Yes! The script consolidates all paginated findings into a single JSON file. Here's how it works:

1. **Accumulates findings in `$allFindings` array** during pagination loop:
   ```powershell
   $allFindings += $json.findings
   ```

2. **After all pages are fetched**, it builds the final consolidated JSON:
   ```powershell
   $finalJson = @{ findings = $allFindings } | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 20 -Compress
   [System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($outputFile, $finalJson, [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8)
   ```

3. **Saves to a single timestamped file** like:
   ```
   C:\Projects\aws-zl\Deployment Guide\vscan\InspectorScan_20251216154502.json
   ```

The final output file will have the structure:
```json
{"findings":[{...finding1...},{...finding2...},...,{...findingN...}]}
```

With all findings from all pages combined into one array.

Ran terminal command: # Try using Start-Process instead

$tokenValue = $global:token

$args = "inspector2 list-findings --region us-west-1 --filter-criteria file://$filterFile --max-results 10 --starting-token `"$tokenValue`" --output json"

$psi = New-Object System.Diagnostics.ProcessStartInfo
$psi.FileName = "aws"
$psi.Arguments = $args
$psi.RedirectStandardOutput = $true
$psi.RedirectStandardError = $true
$psi.UseShellExecute = $false
$p = [System.Diagnostics.Process]::Start($psi)
$output = $p.StandardOutput.ReadToEnd()
$p.WaitForExit()

[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($tempFile, $output, [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8)
Write-Host "Output length: $($output.Length)"

Ran terminal command: # Get CVE IDs from each scanner
$inspectorFile = "C:\Projects\aws-zl\Deployment Guide\vscan\InspectorScan_20251216122929.json"
$grypeFile = "C:\Projects\aws-zl\Deployment Guide\vscan\RawScanResults_20251216101306.json"

$g = Get-Content $grypeFile -Raw | ConvertFrom-Json

# Extract CVE IDs
$inspectorCVEs = $i.findings | ForEach-Object { $_.packageVulnerabilityDetails.vulnerabilityId } | Sort-Object -Unique
$grypeCVEs = $g.matches | ForEach-Object { $_.vulnerability.id } | Sort-Object -Unique

Write-Host "Inspector unique CVEs: $($inspectorCVEs.Count)"
Write-Host "Grype unique CVEs: $($grypeCVEs.Count)"

# Find overlaps
$common = $inspectorCVEs | Where-Object { $grypeCVEs -contains $_ }
Write-Host "Common CVEs: $($common.Count)"

# Inspector-only
$inspectorOnly = $inspectorCVEs | Where-Object { $grypeCVEs -notcontains $_ }
Write-Host "Inspector-only CVEs: $($inspectorOnly.Count)"

# Grype-only
$grypeOnly = $grypeCVEs | Where-Object { $inspectorCVEs -notcontains $_ }
Write-Host "Grype-only CVEs: $($grypeOnly.Count)"

Here's the analysis of the two scan files:

## Comparison Results

| Scanner | Total Findings | Unique CVEs |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| **AWS Inspector** | 37 findings | 37 unique CVEs |
| **Grype** | 181 matches | 135 unique CVEs |

### Overlap Analysis
- **Common CVEs (found by both):** 13
- **Inspector-only CVEs:** 24
- **Grype-only CVEs:** 122

### Why the Differences?

1. **Different Vulnerability Databases:**
   - **Grype** uses anchore's feed which includes NVD, GitHub Advisories, and OS-specific sources
   - **AWS Inspector** uses Amazon's curated vulnerability database plus vendor feeds

2. **Different Detection Scopes:**
   - **Grype** scans all packages in the container (OS, Java, Python, etc.)
   - **AWS Inspector** focuses on OS packages and some application packages

3. **Inspector-only CVEs** (24 CVEs) include older/legacy vulnerabilities like:
   - `CVE-2006-2758` (Jetty directory traversal from 2006!)
   - `CVE-2011-4461` (Jetty hash collision)
   - `CVE-2016-6796` (Tomcat)
   - These are Java application vulnerabilities that Inspector detects through its SBOM analysis

4. **Grype-only CVEs** (122 CVEs) are largely OS-level packages and libraries that Grype is more thorough about scanning

### Summary
Both scanners are working correctly - they just have different detection capabilities and databases. Using **both together** provides the most comprehensive vulnerability coverage. The 13 common CVEs represent high-confidence findings where both tools agree.

**Problem:** When comparing Grype vs Inspector scans, only 1 CVE showed as "unchanged" (common) instead of the expected 13, because the comparison was using `CVE:Package:Version` as the key, and **package names/versions differ between scanners** for the same CVE.

Ran terminal command: aws eks describe-cluster --name usw1-zlps-eks-01 --region us-west-1 --query "cluster.resourcesVpcConfig.vpcId" --output text

Ran terminal command: # List all subnets in the VPC with their AZ and tags
aws ec2 describe-subnets --filters "Name=vpc-id,Values=vpc-07644cdd0224da4f3" --region us-west-1 --query "Subnets[*].[SubnetId,AvailabilityZone,CidrBlock,Tags[?Key=='Name'].Value|[0]]" --output table

Ran terminal command: aws ec2 describe-subnets --filters "Name=vpc-id,Values=vpc-07644cdd0224da4f3" --region us-west-1 --query "Subnets[*].[SubnetId,AvailabilityZone,CidrBlock,Tags[?Key=='Name'].Value|[0]]" --output table

Here's how to determine which subnets to use:

## Subnet Selection for EFS Mount Targets

| Subnet ID | AZ | Type | Use for EFS? |
|-----------|-----|------|--------------|
| `subnet-0c861c54ffa4ddbe6` | us-west-1a | **Private** | ✅ **Yes** |
| `subnet-039bd7f435475c3dc` | us-west-1c | **Private** | ✅ **Yes** |
| `subnet-01c24772a882a3a1b` | us-west-1a | Public | ❌ No |
| `subnet-06eb80d784d985109` | us-west-1c | Public | ❌ No |

### Why Private Subnets?

1. **Security**: EFS traffic stays within the VPC, no internet exposure
2. **Where pods run**: EKS worker nodes run in private subnets
3. **Best practice**: Mount targets should be in the same subnets as the clients accessing them

### Your Current Step 22 Uses:

Looking at the guide, it currently shows:
- `subnet-01c24772a882a3a1b` (us-west-1a) - ⚠️ This is **Public**
- `subnet-06eb80d784d985109` (us-west-1c) - ⚠️ This is **Public**

Would you like me to update Step 22 to use the **correct private subnets** instead?

- `subnet-0c861c54ffa4ddbe6` (us-west-1a Private)
- `subnet-039bd7f435475c3dc` (us-west-1c Private)

jpitzen: Is it possible that there is a need for it in both locations?

GitHub Copilot: You're right to question that! Let me think about the actual use case:

**Step 21b-1** needs:
- VPC ID → to create the EFS security group
- EKS Cluster SG ID → to allow NFS traffic FROM the EKS nodes TO the EFS mount targets

**Step 22a** needs:
- VPC ID → to list subnets and find the private ones

So yes, **VPC ID is needed in both places**, but for **different purposes**. However, since Step 21b comes first, users already have the VPC ID from 21b-1, so Step 22a doesn't need to retrieve it again - they can just **reuse** it.

The current approach is correct: **Get it once in 21b-1, reference it in 22a**. This is better because:

1. **Efficiency** - Don't run the same AWS API call twice
2. **Clarity** - Shows the dependency relationship
3. **Best practice** - Get infrastructure IDs once, reuse them

However, I can see an argument for **keeping both** if you want each step to be **self-contained** (so someone can jump directly to Step 22 without doing 21b). 

**My recommendation:** Keep the current approach (get once, reuse) because:
- Phase 4 steps must be done **in order** anyway (you need the security group before creating mount targets)
- It teaches proper workflow
- Prevents confusion about which VPC ID to use if they differ

Would you prefer to keep it streamlined (current approach) or make each step fully self-contained (restore the duplicate)?

jpitzen: keep

GitHub Copilot: Understood - keeping the current streamlined approach where VPC ID is retrieved once in Step 21b-1 and reused in Step 22a. This maintains proper dependency flow and avoids redundant API calls.

The Phase 4 EFS setup flow is validated and ready to use.

jpitzen: in step 31, what is the <RDS_SD_ID> for us-west-1 deployment?

GitHub Copilot: Read [](file:///c%3A/Projects/aws-zl/Deployment%20Guide/ZLAWSDeploy_Guide.html#3160-3160), lines 3160 to 3230

The `<RDS_SG_ID>` placeholder in Step 31 is populated by the output from **Step 31b**. When you run the command to create the RDS security group, it returns the Security Group ID that you then use in Step 31c.

However, looking at your existing AWS resources and the guide's naming convention (`usw1-zlps-rds-sg`), you can find the actual security group ID by running: 

Ran terminal command: aws ec2 describe-security-groups --filters "Name=group-name,Values=usw1-zlps-rds-sg" --region us-west-1 --query "SecurityGroups[0].GroupId" --output text

The `<RDS_SG_ID>` for your us-west-1 deployment is **`sg-0a0718698768e8015`**.

This is the security group ID that was created in Step 31b and should be used in Step 31c when adding the inbound rule for port 1433.

## Command Format Pattern Used

All converted commands now follow this structure:

```html
<div class="shell-tabs">
    <div class="shell-tab-buttons">
        <button class="shell-tab-btn active" onclick="showShell(this, 'stepXX-ps')">PowerShell</button>
        <button class="shell-tab-btn" onclick="showShell(this, 'stepXX-bash')">Bash</button>
        <button class="shell-tab-btn" onclick="showShell(this, 'stepXX-cmd')">CMD</button>
    </div>
    <div id="stepXX-ps" class="shell-content active">
        <!-- PowerShell with backticks ` -->
    </div>
    <div id="stepXX-bash" class="shell-content">
        <!-- Bash with backslashes \ -->
    </div>
    <div id="stepXX-cmd" class="shell-content">
        <!-- CMD single line with REM comments -->
    </div>
</div>
```

The focus was on **multi-line commands** where shell syntax differs significantly (backslash vs backtick continuation). Single-line AWS CLI commands work identically across all shells and were left as-is to avoid unnecessary bloat. The deployment guide now provides a professional, user-friendly experience for users on any platform!

jpitzen: check the powershell cmd in Step 37: SSM Port Forwarding for Database Access it isn't functioning properly

GitHub Copilot: Read [](file:///c%3A/Projects/aws-zl/Deployment%20Guide/ZLAWSDeploy_Guide.html#3588-3588), lines 3588 to 3625

I can see the issue. The PowerShell command is using escaped backticks (\`") for the JSON which won't work properly in PowerShell. In PowerShell, when you need to pass JSON with quotes to an AWS CLI command, you should use single quotes around the JSON and escape inner quotes differently, or use a here-string. Let me fix this: 

Using "Replace String in File"


```

```
Fixed! The issue was that the PowerShell command was using backtick-escaped quotes (\`") which creates problems in HTML. The correct approach for PowerShell is to:

1. Use **single quotes** around the entire JSON parameter
2. **Escape inner double quotes** with backslashes: `\"` 

This way PowerShell treats the single-quoted string literally and AWS CLI correctly interprets the escaped quotes as JSON. I also added a CMD tab for completeness, which uses the same escaping pattern as PowerShell (double quotes with backslash escapes).

Made changes.

jpitzen: Step 37e: Start Port Forwarding Session is returning a syntax error
 
PS C:\eksdeployment> aws ssm start-session --target "i-0c4a7fcbb2ec5890c" --document-name "AWS-StartPortForwardingSessionToRemoteHost" --parameters '{"host" : ["use1-zlps-msexpsql-01-eks.cqhaqqqiwngv.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com"], "portNumber":["1433"], "localPortNumber":["1433"]}'
>>
 
Error parsing parameter '--parameters': Invalid JSON: Expecting property name enclosed in double quotes: line 1 column 2 (char 1)
JSON received: {host : [use1-zlps-msexpsql-01-eks.cqhaqqqiwngv.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com], portNumber:[1433], localPortNumber:[1433]}

GitHub Copilot: The issue is PowerShell's JSON escaping. When you copy the command from the guide, PowerShell strips the inner quotes. Let me check and fix Step 37e: 

Read [](file:///c%3A/Projects/aws-zl/Deployment%20Guide/ZLAWSDeploy_Guide.html#3760-3760), lines 3760 to 3810

I can see the issue. PowerShell needs the JSON to be properly escaped. Let me fix Step 37e with the correct PowerShell escaping: 

Using "Replace String in File"


```

```
Fixed Step 37e with proper JSON escaping for all shells. For **PowerShell**, use this command with your values:

```powershell
aws ssm start-session `
  --target i-0c4a7fcbb2ec5890c `
  --document-name AWS-StartPortForwardingSessionToRemoteHost `
  --parameters '{\"host\":[\"use1-zlps-msexpsql-01-eks.cqhaqqqiwngv.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com\"],\"portNumber\":[\"1433\"],\"localPortNumber\":[\"1433\"]}' `
  --region us-east-1
```

The key fix: PowerShell requires **escaped quotes** (`\"`) inside the JSON string, just like CMD.

Added **Step 37d: Install Session Manager Plugin** with installation instructions for:
- **Windows**: Direct download or Chocolatey install
- **macOS**: Homebrew or manual install  
- **Linux**: DEB/RPM packages

For Windows, run this in PowerShell as Administrator:

```powershell
Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "https://s3.amazonaws.com/session-manager-downloads/plugin/latest/windows/SessionManagerPluginSetup.exe" `
  -OutFile "$env:USERPROFILE\Downloads\SessionManagerPluginSetup.exe"
```

Then run the installer, **close and reopen your terminal**, and verify:

```powershell
session-manager-plugin
```

You should see the plugin version information. Then retry your SSM port forwarding command.

jpitzen: sqlcmd to test connectivity

GitHub Copilot: Here's the sqlcmd command to test connectivity to your RDS instance through the SSM port forwarding session:

```powershell
# Test basic connectivity
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U admin -P YOUR_ADMIN_PASSWORD -Q "SELECT @@VERSION"

# Or test interactively
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U admin -P YOUR_ADMIN_PASSWORD
```

Once connected interactively, you can run:
```sql
1> SELECT @@VERSION;
2> GO
1> SELECT name FROM sys.databases;
2> GO
1> EXIT
```

**Quick connectivity test (one-liner):**
```powershell
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U admin -P YOUR_ADMIN_PASSWORD -Q "SELECT GETDATE() AS CurrentTime, @@SERVERNAME AS ServerName, DB_NAME() AS CurrentDB"
```

Make sure:
- The SSM port forwarding session from Step 37f is still running
- Replace `YOUR_ADMIN_PASSWORD` with the password you set in Step 32
- The connection uses `localhost,1433` (comma, not colon)

jpitzen: Connection to destination port failed

GitHub Copilot: The "Connection to destination port failed" error means the SSM port forwarding session can't reach your RDS instance. Let's troubleshoot:

**1. Verify the SSM Host can reach RDS:**

First, check if the security groups allow traffic:

```powershell
# Get your SSM Host security group ID
aws ec2 describe-instances --instance-ids i-0c4a7fcbb2ec5890c --region us-east-1 --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].SecurityGroups[0].GroupId' --output text

# Get your RDS security group rules
aws ec2 describe-security-group-rules --filters "Name=group-id,Values=sg-0a0718698768e8015" --region us-east-1 --output table
```

**2. Check if RDS security group allows inbound from SSM Host SG:**

The RDS security group needs to allow port 1433 from the SSM Host's security group:

```powershell
# Add rule if missing (replace <SSM_SG_ID> with your SSM Host's security group)
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress `
  --group-id sg-0a0718698768e8015 `
  --protocol tcp `
  --port 1433 `
  --source-group <SSM_SG_ID> `
  --region us-east-1
```

**3. Verify RDS endpoint is correct:**

```powershell
aws rds describe-db-instances --db-instance-identifier use1-zlps-msexpsql-01-eks --region us-east-1 --query 'DBInstances[0].Endpoint.Address' --output text
```

**4. Test from SSM Host directly:**

You can connect to the SSM Host via Session Manager and test connectivity:

```powershell
aws ssm start-session --target i-0c4a7fcbb2ec5890c --region us-east-1
```

Once connected, run:
```powershell
Test-NetConnection -ComputerName use1-zlps-msexpsql-01-eks.cqhaqqqiwngv.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com -Port 1433
```

**Most likely issue:** The RDS security group doesn't have an inbound rule allowing port 1433 from the SSM Host's security group.

jpitzen: I'll start the session, you test the port connectivity:

>>   --parameters '{\"host\":[\"use1-zlps-msexpsql-01-eks.cqhaqqqiwngv.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com\"],\"portNumber\":[\"1433\"],\"localPortNumber\":[\"1433\"]}' `

If it shows **TcpTestSucceeded: False** ❌, then the issue is the security group rule. Run this to fix it:

```powershell
# Get SSM Host security group
$ssmSgId = aws ec2 describe-instances --instance-ids i-0c4a7fcbb2ec5890c --region us-east-1 --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].SecurityGroups[0].GroupId' --output text

# Add inbound rule to RDS security group
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress `
  --group-id sg-0a0718698768e8015 `
  --protocol tcp `
  --port 1433 `
  --source-group $ssmSgId `
  --region us-east-1
```

What result do you get from `Test-NetConnection`?

jpitzen: This user exists, we're in the us-east-1 region, 

Cannot alter the user 'pfuser', because it does not exist or you do not have permission.

I'm trying to grant it ownership of the ZLDB, the user login was created

GitHub Copilot: The user login exists but the database user doesn't. You need to create the database user mapped to the login, then grant ownership:

```sql
-- Switch to the ZipLipDB database
USE ZipLipDB;
GO

-- Create database user mapped to the login
CREATE USER pfuser FOR LOGIN pfuser;
GO

-- Grant db_owner role
ALTER ROLE db_owner ADD MEMBER pfuser;
GO

-- Verify
SELECT 
    dp.name AS DatabaseUser,
    dp.type_desc AS UserType,
    r.name AS Role
FROM sys.database_principals dp
LEFT JOIN sys.database_role_members drm ON dp.principal_id = drm.member_principal_id
LEFT JOIN sys.database_principals r ON drm.role_principal_id = r.principal_id
WHERE dp.name = 'pfuser';
GO
```

Or as a one-liner via sqlcmd:

```powershell
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U admin -P YOUR_ADMIN_PASSWORD -d ZipLipDB -Q "CREATE USER pfuser FOR LOGIN pfuser; ALTER ROLE db_owner ADD MEMBER pfuser; SELECT name, type_desc FROM sys.database_principals WHERE name = 'pfuser';"
```

**Note:** A SQL Server login (server-level) is different from a database user (database-level). The login exists on the server, but you need to create the user in each database where they need access.

jpitzen: master admin getting a "User does not have permission to perform this action"

GitHub Copilot: SQL Server Express has restrictions on the `admin` user created during RDS setup. Try using the `AUTHORIZATION` syntax instead:

```sql
USE ZipLipDB;
GO

-- Create database user for pfuser login
CREATE USER pfuser FOR LOGIN pfuser;
GO

-- Grant ownership using AUTHORIZATION (works on RDS/Express)
ALTER AUTHORIZATION ON DATABASE::ZipLipDB TO pfuser;
GO

-- Verify ownership
SELECT 
    name AS DatabaseName,
    SUSER_SNAME(owner_sid) AS Owner
FROM sys.databases
WHERE name = 'ZipLipDB';
GO
```

Or via sqlcmd:

```powershell
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U admin -P YOUR_ADMIN_PASSWORD -d ZipLipDB -Q "CREATE USER pfuser FOR LOGIN pfuser; ALTER AUTHORIZATION ON DATABASE::ZipLipDB TO pfuser;"
```

If that still fails, the admin user might not have sufficient privileges. Alternative approach - grant individual permissions:

```sql
USE ZipLipDB;
GO

CREATE USER pfuser FOR LOGIN pfuser;
GO

-- Grant comprehensive permissions
GRANT CONTROL ON DATABASE::ZipLipDB TO pfuser;
GRANT CREATE TABLE TO pfuser;
GRANT CREATE PROCEDURE TO pfuser;
GRANT CREATE VIEW TO pfuser;
GRANT CREATE FUNCTION TO pfuser;
GO
```

**Note:** RDS SQL Server Express has limitations compared to full SQL Server. If `admin` doesn't have permission, you may need to recreate the RDS instance with a different master username or use the permissions-based approach instead of ownership transfer.

jpitzen: how do I check users available?

GitHub Copilot: Here are the commands to check available users at both server and database levels:

**Check Server Logins (server-level):**
```sql
-- List all logins on the server
SELECT 
    name AS LoginName,
    type_desc AS LoginType,
    create_date AS Created,
    is_disabled AS IsDisabled
FROM sys.server_principals
WHERE type IN ('S', 'U')  -- S = SQL Login, U = Windows User
ORDER BY name;
GO
```

**Check Database Users (database-level):**
```sql
-- Switch to your database first
USE ZipLipDB;
GO

-- List all users in the database
SELECT 
    dp.name AS UserName,
    dp.type_desc AS UserType,
    dp.create_date AS Created,
    STRING_AGG(r.name, ', ') AS Roles
FROM sys.database_principals dp
LEFT JOIN sys.database_role_members drm ON dp.principal_id = drm.member_principal_id
LEFT JOIN sys.database_principals r ON drm.role_principal_id = r.principal_id
WHERE dp.type IN ('S', 'U')  -- S = SQL User, U = Windows User
GROUP BY dp.name, dp.type_desc, dp.create_date
ORDER BY dp.name;
GO
```

**Via sqlcmd one-liners:**
```powershell
# Check server logins
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U admin -P YOUR_PASSWORD -Q "SELECT name, type_desc, is_disabled FROM sys.server_principals WHERE type IN ('S', 'U') ORDER BY name"

# Check database users
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U admin -P YOUR_PASSWORD -d ZipLipDB -Q "SELECT name, type_desc FROM sys.database_principals WHERE type IN ('S', 'U') ORDER BY name"

# Check current user and permissions
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U admin -P YOUR_PASSWORD -d ZipLipDB -Q "SELECT USER_NAME() AS CurrentUser, IS_MEMBER('db_owner') AS IsOwner"
```

**Quick check for specific user:**
```powershell
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U admin -P YOUR_PASSWORD -Q "SELECT 'Login exists' WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM sys.server_principals WHERE name = 'pfuser')"
```

jpitzen: The pfuser is listed for the first command.

GitHub Copilot: Good! The login exists at the server level. Now you need to create the database user in ZipLipDB and grant permissions:

```sql
USE ZipLipDB;
GO

-- Create database user mapped to the server login
CREATE USER pfuser FOR LOGIN pfuser;
GO

-- Grant db_owner role
ALTER ROLE db_owner ADD MEMBER pfuser;
GO

-- Verify the user was created and has permissions
SELECT 
    dp.name AS UserName,
    r.name AS RoleMembership
FROM sys.database_principals dp
LEFT JOIN sys.database_role_members drm ON dp.principal_id = drm.member_principal_id
LEFT JOIN sys.database_principals r ON drm.role_principal_id = r.principal_id
WHERE dp.name = 'pfuser';
GO
```

**Via sqlcmd:**
```powershell
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U admin -P YOUR_PASSWORD -d ZipLipDB -Q "CREATE USER pfuser FOR LOGIN pfuser; ALTER ROLE db_owner ADD MEMBER pfuser; SELECT 'User created and granted db_owner' AS Result"
```

This creates the database-level user that maps to the existing server-level login, then grants it db_owner permissions on the ZipLipDB database.

jpitzen: Cannot add the principal 'pfuser', because it does not exist or you do not have permission.

If you get an error about permissions, the RDS `admin` user might not have sufficient privileges on SQL Server Express. Try this workaround:

```powershell
# Connect as admin to master database
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U admin -P YOUR_PASSWORD -d master -Q "ALTER SERVER ROLE sysadmin ADD MEMBER pfuser"
```

This grants `pfuser` sysadmin rights at the server level, which gives full access to all databases including ZipLipDB.

**Alternative - if sysadmin fails, grant specific database permissions:**
```powershell
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U admin -P YOUR_PASSWORD -d ZipLipDB -Q "GRANT CONTROL ON DATABASE::ZipLipDB TO pfuser"
```

What error do you get from the `CREATE USER` command?

```powershell
# List all tables in ZLDB
```

jpitzen: command to create the schema for pfuser

GitHub Copilot: ```powershell
# Create schema owned by pfuser
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U admin -P YOUR_PASSWORD -d ZLDB -Q "CREATE SCHEMA pfuser AUTHORIZATION pfuser"

# Verify schema was created
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U admin -P YOUR_PASSWORD -d ZLDB -Q "SELECT name, USER_NAME(principal_id) AS Owner FROM sys.schemas WHERE name = 'pfuser'"
```

If you want to set `pfuser` schema as the default for the user (instead of dbo):

```powershell
# Change pfuser's default schema to pfuser
sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U admin -P YOUR_PASSWORD -d ZLDB -Q "ALTER USER pfuser WITH DEFAULT_SCHEMA = pfuser"
```

**Note:** If the application expects objects in the `dbo` schema (which is typical), you don't need a separate `pfuser` schema. The user already has `db_owner` permissions with `dbo` as default schema, which should be sufficient for all operations.

jpitzen: C:\Projects\aws-zl\Deployment Guide\vscan>kubectl get secret zldb-secret -n default -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 --decode
'base64' is not recognized as an internal or external command,
operable program or batch file.

GitHub Copilot: In PowerShell on Windows, use this instead:

```powershell
# Decode the secret password
$encoded = kubectl get secret zldb-secret -n default -o jsonpath='{.data.password}'
[System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetString([System.Convert]::FromBase64String($encoded))
```

Or as a one-liner:

```powershell
[System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetString([System.Convert]::FromBase64String((kubectl get secret zldb-secret -n default -o jsonpath='{.data.password}')))
```

The `base64` command is a Linux/Bash utility. Windows PowerShell uses .NET methods for base64 encoding/decoding.

jpitzen: What should this line be, if I'm doing Option 1?

GitHub Copilot: For **Option 1** (copying specific b1140 images), here's the complete PowerShell code:

```powershell
# Variables - customize these for your environment
$SOURCE_REGION = "us-west-1"
$SOURCE_ACCOUNT = "995553364920"
$SOURCE_REPO = "uw1-zlps-ecr-01"

$DEST_REGION = "us-east-1"
$DEST_ACCOUNT = "995553364920"
$DEST_REPO = "ue1-zlps-ecr-01"

# Step 1: Authenticate to SOURCE ECR
aws ecr get-login-password --region $SOURCE_REGION | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin "$SOURCE_ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$SOURCE_REGION.amazonaws.com"

# Step 2: Authenticate to DESTINATION ECR
aws ecr get-login-password --region $DEST_REGION | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin "$DEST_ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$DEST_REGION.amazonaws.com"

# Step 3: Copy specific build 1140 images
foreach ($img in @("zlserver11.1.0-b1140", "zltika11.1.0-b1140", "zlzookeeper11.1.0-b1140", "zlfile11.1.0-b1140")) {
    docker pull "$SOURCE_ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$SOURCE_REGION.amazonaws.com/${SOURCE_REPO}:$img"
    docker tag "$SOURCE_ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$SOURCE_REGION.amazonaws.com/${SOURCE_REPO}:$img" `
               "$DEST_ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$DEST_REGION.amazonaws.com/${DEST_REPO}:$img"
    docker push "$DEST_ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$DEST_REGION.amazonaws.com/${DEST_REPO}:$img"
    Write-Host "Copied: $img" -ForegroundColor Green
}
```

This will copy only the four specific b1140 images you need: zlserver, zltika, zlzookeeper, and zlfile.
